```markdown
---
name: automation-connector-playbooks-github-linear-failure-check
description: >
  Diagnose workflow failures for GitHub and Linear workflows: a connector troubleshooting report. Produces a copy-ready failure report. Triggers: "GitHub and Linear workflows failure-check", /automation-connector-playbooks-github-linear-failure-check.
metadata:
  category: automation
  type: connector
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: medium
  real_world_use_case: Live Connectors & Automations
  connectors: github,linear
  integrations: @github,@linear
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /automation-connector-playbooks-github-linear-failure-check

Diagnose workflow failures for GitHub and Linear workflows: a connector troubleshooting report. Produces a copy-ready failure report. Triggers: "GitHub and Linear workflows failure-check", /automation-connector-playbooks-github-linear-failure-check.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when the GitHub↔Linear sync (native integration, webhook bridge, or team convention) stops being trustworthy: merged PRs no longer close their issues, issues jump to Done with no merge, one merge closes the wrong team's issue, or duplicate linked attachments accumulate on every push. Sync here fails along known seams - magic-word parsing, team-prefix collisions, webhook delivery, and state-mapping - and each has a distinct live-readable signature.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Symptom in one sentence and the last known-good sync
- The sync mechanism as understood (native integration, bot, or convention) and which direction(s) it writes
- One failing example: a PR and the issue it should have affected (links or numbers)
- Repos and Linear team key(s) involved - and whether multiple teams share word-like prefixes (e.g. `API-` and `APP-`) or multiple workspaces touch the same repos
- Audit window (default: since last-known-good)

## Workflow

**Phase 1 - Reproduce with the example (read-only).** Pull the PR: does its body/branch actually contain a well-formed reference (`fixes ABC-123` - not `fixes ABC 123` or a reference only in a commit message the parser ignores)? Was it merged to the branch the sync watches (default branch, usually - merges to release branches commonly don't count)? Pull the issue: state history around the merge time, existing attachments, team prefix.

**Phase 2 - Audit the window (read-only).** Enumerate merged scoped PRs with references, and scoped issues with state changes. Bucket: healthy (merge → close within minutes), `merge-no-close` (losses), `close-no-merge` (issues transitioned by something other than code - human or misfire), `wrong-target` (reference resolved to an unintended issue - prefix collision signature), and `duplicate-attachments` (same PR linked N times - webhook redelivery without idempotency).

**Phase 3 - Check the seams (read-only).** Reference syntax: sample the misses - is there a consistent malformation (lowercase prefix, missing colon, reference in commit-not-body)? Branch scope: are all misses merges to non-default branches? Team collision: do wrong-target cases share a colliding prefix? Auth/installation: is the integration installed on the specific repo (org-level installs with per-repo grants miss new repos - check whether failures cluster in recently created repos)? State mapping: does the sync target a state name that was renamed in Linear's workflow settings?

**Phase 4 - Report, then propose remediation. Stop before writes.** Deliver the report. Remediation writes - closing the missed issues (with merge evidence), removing duplicate attachments, correcting wrong-target closures (reopen wrongly closed, close rightly resolved) - go to an approval queue. Reopen rows are sensitive: they resurrect issues on someone's board; flag who gets notified. Every close row re-checks current state first.

**Re-run behavior.** Diagnosis is read-only and repeatable. Remediation rows verify state at execution time, so approving a stale queue cannot regress issues someone already fixed.

**Fallbacks.** If either connector is unreachable, lead with that; report the reachable side and mark the rest unverified. Never reconstruct the missing side from the sync's own logs alone - the logs are a suspect in this investigation.

## Connector access (required)

This skill cannot run from text alone. It must call live integrations.

1. Confirm each required app is enabled and authenticated in the current AI tool.
2. Discover the exact available connector tools first; do not assume the example names exist.
3. Use read-only calls before proposing writes. For sends, deletes, publishes, refunds, or updates, show a review table and wait for explicit approval.

### Required integrations

- **@github** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `github`.
- **@linear** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `linear`.

### If a connector is missing

Stop and tell the user which app is missing. Give setup steps for their tool, then ask them to retry after authentication. Do not substitute generic web search for private workspace data.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- Diagnosis never writes. Remediation verifies current state per row at execution time; reopen rows explicitly name whose board they will change.

## Output format

**Symptom & last known good**

**Failure report**

| Check | Expected | Observed (evidence link/ID) | Verdict |

**Loss accounting** - healthy / merge-no-close / close-no-merge / wrong-target / duplicate-attachment counts, with links.

**Root cause** - the confirmed mechanism (e.g. "integration not installed on repo `payments-v2`, created after the org grant; all misses cluster there"), runners-up with the read that would settle each.

**Remediation queue** (writes; requires approval)

| # | Action | Target | Evidence | State re-check | Notifies | Risk |

**Run summary**

| Step | App | Produced | Status |

## Do

- Sample the exact reference text of misses - malformed magic words are the most common and most fixable cause
- Check whether failures cluster by repo age, branch, or team prefix before blaming the platform
- Distinguish "sync never fired" from "sync fired and was rejected" - attachment traces and timestamps tell them apart
- Cite PR numbers, commit SHAs, and issue identifiers in every evidence cell

## Don't

- Don't treat `close-no-merge` as sync failure without checking for a human actor in the issue history
- Don't propose reopening an issue without quoting the evidence it was wrongly closed
- Don't fix the backlog and the root cause in one approval - separate the queue from the recommendation
- Don't assume the integration covers every repo because it works in one

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The failing example traced across both apps with its reference text quoted
- [ ] All five buckets counted over the window with links
- [ ] Clustering (repo, branch, prefix) checked before naming a cause
- [ ] Remediation rows re-check state and name notified parties; none executed
- [ ] Run summary shows step, app, produced, status
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /automation-connector-playbooks-github-linear-failure-check or describe the task from the skill description.

# /automation-connector-playbooks-calendar-email-read-only-digest

Summarize live workspace activity for calendar and email workflows: a read-only digest from connected apps. Produces a copy-ready workspace digest. Triggers: "calendar and email workflows read-only-digest", /automation-connector-playbooks-calendar-email-read-only-digest.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this for the morning or Sunday-evening sweep: what is on the calendar for the coming window, which meetings have live email context (agenda sent? attendee replied with a conflict? pre-read attached?), and which past meetings still owe a follow-up that never left the outbox. Calendar shows the skeleton; Gmail shows whether each meeting is actually prepared. This playbook reads both and cross-references; it never sends, declines, or reschedules anything.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Calendar scope: which calendar(s) - primary only, or shared/team calendars too
- Window: look-ahead (default next 5 business days) and look-back for unsent follow-ups (default 7 days)
- Which meetings matter: external-attendee meetings only, or everything above N attendees; ignore all-day/OOO blocks?
- Gmail matching hints: does the team use subject conventions ("[Prep]", "Agenda:") or an attendee-email match only
- Timezone to report in, if the user books across zones

## Workflow

**Phase 1 - Read the calendar.** Pull events in the look-ahead window: title, start/end (with timezone), organizer, attendee list with response status (accepted / declined / no reply), location or meeting link, and description text. Also pull the look-back window's external meetings for follow-up checking.

**Phase 2 - Read Gmail.** For each upcoming event, search mail linking to it: messages to/from its attendees within the window, subject overlap with the event title, and invite-update messages. For each look-back meeting, search sent mail for a follow-up (to those attendees, after the event end) and drafts that were started but never sent.

**Phase 3 - Cross-match and flag.** Per upcoming meeting flag: no-agenda (nothing matching sent to attendees), key-attendee-silent (organizer-critical attendee hasn't accepted), conflict-mail (an attendee emailed about rescheduling but the event is unchanged - the classic stale-data trap), and double-booked slots. Per past meeting flag: follow-up-missing and follow-up-stuck-in-drafts. Match attendees by email address, not display name - two "John S." entries are different people until their addresses match.

**Phase 4 - Compose the digest.** Chronological, timezone-explicit, every flag linked to its event and message. Zero writes: no RSVP changes, no drafts created, nothing sent.

**Re-run behavior.** Fully idempotent. Re-state the as-of time each run - RSVPs and inboxes shift hourly, and yesterday's digest quietly becomes wrong.

**Fallbacks.** If Gmail is unavailable, deliver a calendar-only digest and mark the preparation column "unverified". If the calendar is unavailable, offer the reverse (meeting-related threads by attendee) but say plainly that times and RSVPs are unconfirmed.

## Connector access (required)

This skill cannot run from text alone. It must call live integrations.

1. Confirm each required app is enabled and authenticated in the current AI tool.
2. Discover the exact available connector tools first; do not assume the example names exist.
3. Use read-only calls before proposing writes. For sends, deletes, publishes, refunds, or updates, show a review table and wait for explicit approval.

### Required integrations

- **@calendar** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `calendar`.
- **@gmail** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `gmail`.

### If a connector is missing

Stop and tell the user which app is missing. Give setup steps for their tool, then ask them to retry after authentication. Do not substitute generic web search for private workspace data.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- This playbook is read-only end to end. Requests to draft, send, accept, or reschedule mid-run become a new task with its own review table.

## Output format

**Scope & window** - calendars, look-ahead/look-back, timezone, as-of timestamp.

**Upcoming meetings**

| When | Meeting | Attendee status | Prep signal | Flags | Links |

**Owed follow-ups**

| Met on | Meeting | Follow-up state | Evidence | Link |

**Needs attention** - conflict-mail items first (someone asked to move a meeting that hasn't moved), then double-bookings, then no-agenda externals.

**Run summary**

| Step | App | Produced | Status |

## Do

- Put every time in the stated timezone and say which zone it is
- Rank conflict-mail above everything - mail contradicting the calendar is the costliest miss
- Match people by email address across the two apps, never by display name
- State the as-of time; RSVP columns go stale within hours

## Don't

- Don't infer "prepared" from any attendee email - an agenda is a sent message to the attendees, not a reply in the thread
- Don't count a declined-but-still-on-calendar attendee as attending
- Don't fold recurring-event instances into one row when their RSVP or prep state differs
- Don't create drafts "to save time" - the read-only guarantee is the product

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every meeting row links to its event; every flag links to its evidence message
- [ ] Timezone and as-of time are explicit
- [ ] Conflict-mail, double-booking, and owed-follow-up flags were all checked, not just one
- [ ] Zero write calls (no drafts, no RSVP changes) were made
- [ ] Run summary shows step, app, produced, status
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /automation-connector-playbooks-calendar-email-read-only-digest or describe the task from the skill description.

# /automation-connector-playbooks-calendar-email-approval-queue

Prepare changes for approval for calendar and email workflows: a safe review queue before any write action. Produces a copy-ready approval queue. Triggers: "calendar and email workflows approval-queue", /automation-connector-playbooks-calendar-email-approval-queue.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this to turn calendar reality into queued email actions - and never the reverse without sign-off. Typical batch: draft follow-up emails for yesterday's external meetings, draft agenda/pre-read sends for next week's meetings that have none, and propose invite updates where an attendee's email asked for a new time. Every send and every event change sits in a review queue first; the user approves rows, not vibes. Meeting invites and reschedules are especially unforgiving - an approved-then-wrong invite update notifies every attendee.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Which batch: post-meeting follow-ups, pre-meeting agendas, reschedule proposals, or a mix
- Calendar scope and window (default: follow-ups from the last 3 business days, agendas for the next 5)
- Which meetings qualify (external attendees only? specific calendar? skip 1:1s?)
- Voice and template hints for drafts: sign-off, length, any standard agenda format
- Dedupe marker: how a handled meeting is recognized (a sent follow-up to those attendees after the event; a draft label like `queued-followup`)
- Hard rule confirmation: send immediately after approval, or create drafts only for the user to send

## Workflow

**Phase 1 - Read (no writes).** Pull qualifying events. For follow-ups: capture attendees, event notes/description, and search sent mail to confirm no follow-up already went to those attendees after the event ended - already-sent meetings become `skip` rows. For agendas: confirm nothing agenda-like was already sent. For reschedules: collect the attendee emails requesting a change and the exact quoted constraint ("can't do Thursday").

**Phase 2 - Compose proposals (still no writes).** Draft each email in full - recipients, subject, body - grounded only in the event data and quoted thread content. For reschedule rows, propose specific new slots read from actual calendar free/busy in the window, never invented availability.

**Phase 3 - Present the queue. Stop.** One row per action: `draft follow-up`, `send agenda`, `update event time`, with recipients, a body preview, and risk (event updates notify all N attendees). Wait for explicit approval of `apply all` or row numbers. Nothing sends, no event mutates, before that.

**Phase 4 - Execute approved rows.** Create drafts or send per the agreed mode; apply the dedupe marker; for event updates, re-read the event afterward to verify the new time and note that attendees were notified. Report per-row status. If a row fails (bounced address, event edit rejected), continue the rest and report the failure verbatim.

**Re-run behavior.** The Phase-1 sent-mail check plus the dedupe marker make re-runs additive, not duplicative: a meeting whose follow-up went out - by this tool or by the human - shows as `skip (already handled)`.

**Fallbacks.** If the calendar connector is missing, this playbook cannot verify events or availability - offer only quoted-thread-based drafts, clearly labeled unverified. If Gmail is missing, deliver the composed follow-up/agenda texts inline for manual sending, with the recipient list per item.

## Connector access (required)

This skill cannot run from text alone. It must call live integrations.

1. Confirm each required app is enabled and authenticated in the current AI tool.
2. Discover the exact available connector tools first; do not assume the example names exist.
3. Use read-only calls before proposing writes. For sends, deletes, publishes, refunds, or updates, show a review table and wait for explicit approval.

### Required integrations

- **@calendar** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `calendar`.
- **@gmail** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `gmail`.

### If a connector is missing

Stop and tell the user which app is missing. Give setup steps for their tool, then ask them to retry after authentication. Do not substitute generic web search for private workspace data.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- Event updates and email sends are never bundled into one approval line - each notifying action is its own row.
- Default to drafts over sends unless the user explicitly chose send mode before the queue was built.

## Output format

**Scope** - batch type, window, qualifying rule, dedupe marker.

**Approval queue**

| # | Action | Meeting / thread | Recipients or attendees | Preview | Risk | Duplicate check |

**- approval checkpoint - nothing below runs until approved -**

**Execution report** (after approval)

| # | Action | Result (draft/sent/event link) | Status |

**Run summary**

| Step | App | Produced | Status |

## Do

- Verify "no follow-up sent yet" against sent mail, not memory - humans send follow-ups outside the tool
- Propose reschedule slots from real free/busy reads, quoting the attendee's constraint verbatim
- Call out attendee-notification blast radius on every event-update row
- Keep skipped rows visible with their reason

## Don't

- Don't send anything in the same turn that read the data - the queue is mandatory even for one item
- Don't update a recurring event's series when the request was about one instance
- Don't invent attendee availability or paraphrase their constraint into something stronger
- Don't retry a failed send blindly on re-run; surface it - the address may be wrong, and three retries mean three emails if it half-worked

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every send/update appeared as a queue row with preview and risk before execution
- [ ] Sent-mail dedupe check ran for every follow-up/agenda candidate
- [ ] Reschedule proposals cite real free/busy and quote the requester
- [ ] Partial failures reported per row; unaffected rows completed
- [ ] Run summary shows step, app, produced, status
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /automation-connector-playbooks-calendar-email-approval-queue or describe the task from the skill description.

The failure it prevents: the cover attends a meeting cold, or a thread with a deadline goes unanswered for two weeks.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Absence window (first and last day out) and who is covering - one person or split by area
- Calendar scope: which calendars; include recurring internal meetings or externals only
- Disposition defaults: which meeting types the user wants attended vs declined vs left untouched
- Gmail scope: labels/queries for the work being covered, plus VIP senders who must never wait
- Delivery: inline brief only, or also email it to the cover (a send, requiring approval)

## Workflow

**Phase 1 - Read the calendar across the absence window.** Every event: title, dates (timezone-explicit), attendees and their roles (organizer / required / optional), recurrence, and description. Separate: meetings the user organizes (these need an owner or a cancellation - they cannot just be ignored) from meetings merely attended.

**Phase 2 - Read Gmail for context.** For each significant meeting, pull the latest agreement from its thread: last decision, open question, promised item. Independently sweep scoped labels for threads with pending replies or deadlines falling inside the window - these are commitments even without a meeting attached. Match threads to meetings by attendee addresses and subject tokens, never display names.

**Phase 3 - Build dispositions.** For every organized meeting propose: hand to cover / cancel occurrence(s) / keep (self-running). For attended meetings: cover attends / send regrets / monitor notes. Attach the email context so the cover reads one brief, not forty threads. Mark anything ambiguous "confirm with owner" rather than guessing its importance.

**Phase 4 - Deliver, then checkpoint for writes.** The brief itself is inline output. If the user wants dispositions executed (cancel these, invite the cover to those) or the brief emailed to the cover, list each as a review-queue row - event changes notify attendees; the send has recipients - and wait for approval. Before inviting the cover to a meeting, check they are not already on it (prior run or manual add) to avoid duplicate invites.

**Re-run behavior.** Reads are idempotent. Executed dispositions are re-checked before proposing again - an already-cancelled event or already-added cover shows `done` rather than being re-queued.

**Fallbacks.** If Gmail is unavailable, deliver the calendar dispositions with the context column marked "unverified - connector unavailable". If the calendar is unavailable, stop: an email-only handoff misstates the core commitments, and half a brief reads as whole.

## Connector access (required)

This skill cannot run from text alone. It must call live integrations.

1. Confirm each required app is enabled and authenticated in the current AI tool.
2. Discover the exact available connector tools first; do not assume the example names exist.
3. Use read-only calls before proposing writes. For sends, deletes, publishes, refunds, or updates, show a review table and wait for explicit approval.

### Required integrations

- **@calendar** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `calendar`.
- **@gmail** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `gmail`.

### If a connector is missing

Stop and tell the user which app is missing. Give setup steps for their tool, then ask them to retry after authentication. Do not substitute generic web search for private workspace data.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- Cancelling events, adding the cover to invites, and emailing the brief all notify other people - each is its own approval row, never bundled.

## Output format

**Handoff brief**

- **Coverage** - who is out, when, who covers what, as-of timestamp
- **Organized meetings** - table: | When | Meeting | Attendees | Disposition | Email context (link) |
- **Attended meetings** - same columns, cover's action instead of disposition
- **Live threads landing during absence** - | Thread | Counterpart | Commitment / deadline | Suggested handling | Link |
- **VIP rules** - who gets a same-day reply and what to say
- **Confirm-with-owner list** - everything the run could not classify confidently

**Proposed writes** (only if executing): | # | Action | Event/recipients | Notifies | Status |

**Run summary**

| Step | App | Produced | Status |

## Do

- Treat organizer-owned meetings as the priority - unowned recurring meetings during an absence rot visibly
- Quote the last email agreement per meeting; the cover needs the words, not a vibe
- Keep all times timezone-explicit across the whole window, including DST transitions inside it
- Date-stamp the brief and note it reflects that moment's calendar

## Don't

- Don't classify a meeting's importance from its title - use attendee mix and thread activity, or send it to confirm-with-owner
- Don't propose cancelling a whole recurring series to cover a two-week absence
- Don't email the brief or alter any event without its own approved row
- Don't let a thread deadline inside the window hide because it has no meeting attached

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every organized meeting has an explicit disposition or sits in confirm-with-owner
- [ ] Every disposition row links its email context
- [ ] Deadline-bearing threads without meetings were swept and listed
- [ ] All notifying actions (cancels, invites, sends) queued for separate approval, with duplicate-invite checks
- [ ] Run summary shows step, app, produced, status
```

---
name: automation-connector-playbooks-calendar-email-failure-check
description: >
  Diagnose workflow failures for calendar and email workflows: a connector troubleshooting report. Produces a copy-ready failure report. Triggers: "calendar and email workflows failure-check", /automation-connector-playbooks-calendar-email-failure-check.
metadata:

allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /automation-connector-playbooks-calendar-email-failure-check

Diagnose workflow failures for calendar and email workflows: a connector troubleshooting report. Produces a copy-ready failure report. Triggers: "calendar and email workflows failure-check", /automation-connector-playbooks-calendar-email-failure-check.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when a calendar-email automation misbehaves: invite emails arrive but events never appear, reminder or follow-up emails stopped going out, events duplicate on the calendar after every sync, or an attendee says "I replied to move it" while the event sits unmoved. These flows break along predictable seams - timezone math, recurring-event expansion, reply-parsing, and one-way sync loops - and this playbook checks each seam with live reads before naming a cause.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Symptom in one sentence, and the last time it worked
- What the automation is supposed to do (its trigger and its write) as the user understands it
- One concrete failing example: a specific event or message (link, or title + date) that behaved wrongly
- Which calendar(s) and which mailbox are involved; any third calendar system in the chain (a booking tool, another provider) worth knowing about
- Audit window (default: since last-known-good)

## Workflow

**Phase 1 - Reproduce with the example (read-only).** Trace the failing item across both apps: does the source message/event actually match the automation's trigger (run the search live)? Does the counterpart artifact exist under a slightly different key - same title at a different time (timezone shift), a single instance where a series was expected, or the event on a different calendar than assumed?

**Phase 2 - Audit the window (read-only).** Enumerate trigger-matching items and their expected counterparts; bucket into: healthy pairs, source-without-counterpart (losses), counterpart-duplicated (each sync pass re-creates the event - the signature of a missing dedupe key, usually the invite's UID being ignored), and mutated-but-not-propagated (reply asked for change, event unchanged). Count each bucket with links.

**Phase 3 - Check the seams (read-only).** Timezones: compare the source's stated time against the created event's UTC offset - a consistent N-hour error is a zone bug, not randomness. Recurrence: does the failure hit only recurring instances or series edits? Auth: can this session read both apps now; a half-expired grant yields exactly one-sided behavior. Identity: are attendee matches done on email address - display-name matching breaks on the first "J. Smith". Calendar target: was the destination calendar renamed, unshared, or is the automation writing to primary by default.

**Phase 4 - Report, then propose remediation. Stop before writes.** Deliver the failure report. Fixes that write - deleting duplicate events, re-creating lost ones, sending catch-up emails - go into a remediation queue for explicit approval. Deleting calendar events notifies attendees: flag that on every such row. Re-creation rows re-check for existing events first so approving twice cannot double-book.

**Re-run behavior.** Diagnosis is read-only and repeatable; each run restates its as-of time since the calendar keeps moving under audit.

**Fallbacks.** If either connector is unreachable, that is finding #1: report the reachable side's evidence, mark the other side's cells "unverified", and do not guess across the gap.

## Connector access (required)

This skill cannot run from text alone. It must call live integrations.

1. Confirm each required app is enabled and authenticated in the current AI tool.
2. Discover the exact available connector tools first; do not assume the example names exist.
3. Use read-only calls before proposing writes. For sends, deletes, publishes, refunds, or updates, show a review table and wait for explicit approval.

### Required integrations

- **@calendar** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `calendar`.
- **@gmail** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `gmail`.

### If a connector is missing

Stop and tell the user which app is missing. Give setup steps for their tool, then ask them to retry after authentication. Do not substitute generic web search for private workspace data.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- Diagnosis never writes. Remediation rows that delete or modify events state their attendee-notification blast radius; duplicate-event cleanup proposes the minimal set to remove and keeps one canonical event.

## Output format

**Symptom & last known good**

**Failure report**

| Check | Expected | Observed (evidence link/ID) | Verdict |

**Loss accounting** - healthy / missing counterpart / duplicated / unpropagated-change counts over the window, with links.

**Root cause** - most probable mechanism (e.g. "events written in UTC, source times parsed as local - constant +5h offset"), with runners-up and the read that would settle each.

**Remediation queue** (writes; requires approval)

| # | Action | Target event/message | Notifies attendees? | Duplicate check | Risk |

**Run summary**

| Step | App | Produced | Status |

## Do

- Look for the constant-offset signature before calling anything "random" - timezone bugs masquerade as flakiness
- Test recurring and single events separately; most sync code handles only one correctly
- Verify the destination calendar's identity and sharing state before blaming parsing
- Cite message IDs and event IDs in every evidence cell

## Don't

- Don't accept "the automation is on" as evidence its trigger matches - run the query live
- Don't propose deleting "duplicate" events matched on title alone; verify time and attendee identity too
- Don't remediate in the diagnosis turn, even for a single obvious duplicate
- Don't ignore mutated-but-not-propagated items - a reply-requested change that never landed is the failure users feel most

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The failing example was traced end to end with live reads on both sides
- [ ] All four buckets counted over the stated window, with links
- [ ] Root cause names a mechanism and the evidence that confirmed it
- [ ] Every remediation row shows notification blast radius and a duplicate check; none executed
- [ ] Run summary shows step, app, produced, status
```

Best fit: a recurring "what needs my attention across mail and HubSpot" briefing that a rep can act on directly, or feed into the approval-queue playbook when writes are wanted.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Digest window (default: since yesterday for daily runs, 7 days for weekly)
- Mail scope: which mailbox/labels count as customer traffic, and senders/domains to exclude (newsletters, internal, automated)
- CRM scope: which pipelines and deal stages are "active work", and whether only the user's owned records or the whole team's are in scope
- Attention thresholds: what counts as aging (default: inbound unanswered > 2 business days; deal stuck in stage > 14 days)
- Delivery form: chat only, or also a paste-ready block for the user's notes/standup

## Workflow

1. **Read mail.** Pull threads in scope for the window: sender, subject, last-message direction, date, and a one-line gist per thread. Classify each as: needs-reply (inbound, unanswered), waiting-on-them (outbound, no response), or resolved/informational.
2. **Read HubSpot.** Pull active-stage deals (stage, amount, owner, stage-entry date, last activity date) and contacts with activity in the window (new notes, tasks due/overdue, lifecycle changes).
3. **Join the two sides** by email address (domain → company fallback) so each mail thread carries its CRM context (deal stage, owner) and each deal carries its latest mail signal (last inbound/outbound date).
4. **Compute the attention lists** against the thresholds: unanswered inbound ranked oldest-first with the deal it touches; deals past the stuck-in-stage threshold with their last mail contact; overdue CRM tasks whose contact has since emailed (context changed under the task).
5. **Flag the one-sided items** - the digest's distinctive value: threads with active customer mail but no CRM match or no logged activity (invisible to the CRM), and active deals with zero mail traffic in the window (alive on paper only). Report them as observations, not repairs.
6. **Assemble the digest** in reading order: needs-reply first, then deal movement (entered/exited stages this window), then aging items, then one-sided flags, then a short "quiet but fine" line so silence is confirmed rather than assumed. Every line carries its ID/link (message ID or thread link, HubSpot record link) so the user can jump straight in.
7. **Close with suggested next actions** phrased as options (reply to X, review deal Y, log thread Z) - explicitly noting that executing any of them is outside this read-only run.

**Re-run behavior.** Naturally idempotent - nothing is written. Use stable IDs on every line so consecutive digests can be compared; when the previous digest is available in context, mark items that also appeared last time as `carried over`.

**Fallbacks.** If one connector is unavailable, deliver the other side's half-digest with an explicit banner naming what is missing and which sections are therefore blind; do not fill the gap with guesses or web search. If both are unavailable, stop after the connector-setup guidance.

## Connector access (required)

This skill cannot run from text alone. It must call live integrations.

1. Confirm each required app is enabled and authenticated in the current AI tool.
2. Discover the exact available connector tools first; do not assume the example names exist.
3. Use read-only calls before proposing writes. For sends, deletes, publishes, refunds, or updates, show a review table and wait for explicit approval.

- **@hubspot** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `hubspot`.
- **@gmail** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `gmail`.

### If a connector is missing

Stop and tell the user which app is missing. Give setup steps for their tool, then ask them to retry after authentication. Do not substitute generic web search for private workspace data.

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- This playbook performs no writes in either app under any circumstances; anything actionable is handed off as a suggestion.

## Output format

**Scope** - window, mail scope, pipelines, thresholds.

**Needs reply** - | Thread (link/ID) | From | Waiting since | Deal context | Gist |

**Deal movement** - | Deal (link) | Stage change | Amount | Last mail contact |

**Aging** - unanswered and stuck items past thresholds, oldest first.

**One-sided flags** - mail-without-CRM and CRM-without-mail observations, each with IDs.

**Quiet-but-fine** - one line confirming what was checked and found healthy.

**Suggested next actions** - options only; nothing executed.

**Run summary**

| Step | App | Produced | Status |

## Do

- Join every mail item to its CRM context and vice versa - the join is the digest's whole point
- Rank by customer wait time, not by CRM record recency
- Put a link/ID on every line so the user can jump without searching
- Confirm the healthy remainder explicitly; an empty section and an unchecked section must look different

## Don't

- Don't write anything - no labels, no logged notes, no drafts, even if asked mid-run (route that to the approval-queue playbook)
- Don't let newsletters and automated senders inflate the needs-reply list
- Don't merge the two one-sided flag types; unlogged mail and mail-silent deals have different owners
- Don't present a half-digest as complete when one connector was down

- [ ] Both apps were read and joined on email/domain keys, with one-sided items flagged
- [ ] Every line carries a stable ID or link into the source app
- [ ] Attention lists honor the stated thresholds and rank oldest-first
- [ ] Zero writes occurred; next actions are phrased as options
- [ ] Run summary table shows step, app, produced artifact, status for the whole run
```

```markdown
---
name: automation-connector-playbooks-hubspot-mail-approval-queue
description: >
  Prepare changes for approval for CRM and mail workflows: a safe review queue before any write action. Produces a copy-ready approval queue. Triggers: "CRM and mail workflows approval-queue", /automation-connector-playbooks-hubspot-mail-approval-queue.
metadata:
  category: automation

  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: medium
  real_world_use_case: Live Connectors & Automations

allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /automation-connector-playbooks-hubspot-mail-approval-queue

Prepare changes for approval for CRM and mail workflows: a safe review queue before any write action. Produces a copy-ready approval queue. Triggers: "CRM and mail workflows approval-queue", /automation-connector-playbooks-hubspot-mail-approval-queue.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when customer email has outrun the CRM: real conversations sit in the mailbox while HubSpot contacts show stale properties, deals sit in old stages, and nothing is logged. The playbook reads recent mail, derives the CRM updates it implies - log the conversation as a note, update a contact property, create a follow-up task, flag a deal whose stage no longer matches reality - and queues every one as a proposed write. Nothing touches HubSpot until the user approves specific rows. Best fit: a weekly CRM-hygiene run where the mailbox is the source of truth and HubSpot needs to catch up safely.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Mail scope: label, search query, or sender/domain list, plus a date window (default 14 days)
- Which HubSpot objects are in play: contacts only, or also companies, deals, and tasks
- The update policy: what kinds of changes may be proposed (notes/engagements, property updates, new tasks, new contacts) and which properties are off-limits (e.g. lifecycle stage owned by another automation)
- Deal-stage rule: whether stage *changes* may be proposed at all, or only flagged for a human (default: flag only)
- The processed marker: how handled threads are recognized on re-run (a mail label, or presence of a logged note carrying the message ID)

## Workflow

1. **Read mail (no writes).** Pull threads in scope, excluding those already carrying the processed marker. For each, capture: message ID, thread subject, sender address, date, and a two-line factual summary of what the customer said or asked.
2. **Match to CRM (still read-only).** Look up each sender in HubSpot by email address; fall back to domain → company when the individual is unknown. Record contact/company/deal IDs for matches. Non-matches become "new contact?" candidates, never auto-created.
3. **Dedupe against existing CRM records.** Before proposing a logged note, search the matched contact's recent notes/engagements for the message ID or same-day same-subject entry. Before proposing a task, check for an open task with the same subject on the same object. Existing artifact → row becomes `skip (already logged)` with a link. This is what makes re-runs idempotent.
4. **Derive proposals.** Per matched thread, propose the minimal writes the email justifies: a note containing the summary + message ID, a property update quoted from the email (e.g. new phone number, changed role - quote the source line), a follow-up task with owner and due date, or a deal-stage *flag* ("customer says they signed; deal still in Negotiation"). Every proposal cites its evidence.
5. **Present the queue. Stop here.** One row per proposed write with object, field-level before → after where applicable, evidence, and risk note. Wait for explicit approval - `apply all` or row numbers. No approval, no writes.
6. **Execute approved rows only.** Write each approved change, then verify by re-reading the object before reporting success. Apply the processed marker to a thread only after all its rows verify - a failed write must leave the thread unmarked so the next run retries it.
7. **Report.** Per-row execution status with result IDs/links, then the run summary table. Partial failures are listed with the connector error verbatim and leave a resumable state.

**Re-run behavior.** Safe by construction: processed markers exclude handled threads, and step 3's artifact check catches anything re-found - skips are reported, not silently dropped.

**Fallbacks.** If HubSpot is unavailable, still deliver the read side inline: per-thread summaries with the exact updates a human should make, formatted for manual entry. If mail is unavailable, stop - there is no intake. If a single write fails mid-run, finish remaining rows and report the failure verbatim.

## Connector access (required)

This skill cannot run from text alone. It must call live integrations.

1. Confirm each required app is enabled and authenticated in the current AI tool.
2. Discover the exact available connector tools first; do not assume the example names exist.
3. Use read-only calls before proposing writes. For sends, deletes, publishes, refunds, or updates, show a review table and wait for explicit approval.

### Required integrations

- **@hubspot** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `hubspot`.
- **@gmail** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `gmail`.

### If a connector is missing

Stop and tell the user which app is missing. Give setup steps for their tool, then ask them to retry after authentication. Do not substitute generic web search for private workspace data.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- Never execute a write in the same phase that discovered the data - the queue table between read and write is mandatory even for a single item.
- Deal-stage changes and new-contact creation default to flag-only; they execute only when the user has explicitly enabled them and approved the specific row.

## Output format

**Scope** - mail query, window, objects in play, update policy.

**Approval queue**

| # | Object (contact/company/deal/task) | Proposed write (before → after) | Evidence (msg ID + quoted line) | Duplicate check | Risk |

**- approval checkpoint - nothing below runs until approved -**

**Execution report** (after approval)

| # | Write | Result ID/link | Status |

**Run summary**

| Step | App | Produced | Status |

## Do

- Quote the exact email line that justifies each property change - evidence, not inference
- Show `skip (already logged)` rows explicitly; silent skips erode trust in the queue
- Verify every write by re-reading the object before marking its row done
- Keep the processed marker strictly after write verification so failures self-heal on re-run

## Don't

- Don't create contacts or move deal stages without an explicit per-row approval under an explicit policy
- Don't overwrite a filled CRM property with an inferred value - conflicting values become a flag row, not a write
- Don't log the full email body into the CRM when a summary + message ID suffices; mailboxes hold private context CRM viewers shouldn't inherit
- Don't mark a thread processed when any of its writes failed

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every proposed write appeared as a queue row with evidence before anything executed
- [ ] Sender→CRM matching used email address with domain fallback, and non-matches were never auto-created
- [ ] Duplicate check ran against existing notes/tasks for every candidate row
- [ ] Processed-marker ordering guarantees failed writes are retried next run
- [ ] Run summary table shows step, app, produced artifact, status for the whole run
```

```markdown
---

description: >
  Create a cross-app handoff for CRM and mail workflows: a handoff package across tools and teams. Produces a copy-ready handoff brief. Triggers: "CRM and mail workflows handoff-brief", /automation-connector-playbooks-hubspot-mail-handoff-brief.

allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /automation-connector-playbooks-hubspot-mail-handoff-brief

Create a cross-app handoff for CRM and mail workflows: a handoff package across tools and teams. Produces a copy-ready handoff brief. Triggers: "CRM and mail workflows handoff-brief", /automation-connector-playbooks-hubspot-mail-handoff-brief.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when someone must email a customer whose story lives in HubSpot: an account handover to a new owner, a renewal or escalation email, a re-engagement after silence. Writing that email cold means re-reading months of CRM history; writing it uninformed means contradicting what the last rep promised. This playbook reads the contact/deal record - properties, stage history, logged notes, open tickets and tasks, recent engagements - and packages it into a structured brief delivered as a *draft* in the mailbox: context summary on top, suggested message below, nothing sent. Best fit: the recipient of the handoff opens one draft and has everything.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The subject of the handoff: which contact, company, or deal (name, email, or HubSpot ID)
- The handoff purpose: new-owner takeover, renewal, escalation, re-engagement - this sets what the brief emphasizes
- Who receives the brief: the draft's intended sender (the new owner) and whether the draft addresses the customer directly or is an internal briefing email
- Sensitivity rules: internal-only notes that must not leak into anything customer-facing (default: no internal note text enters the customer-facing portion)
- The freshness bar: how recent the pulled context must be, and whether older history is summarized or linked

## Workflow

1. **Resolve the subject.** Look up the contact/deal in HubSpot; if the identifier is ambiguous (multiple contacts at one company, multiple open deals), list the candidates and ask - a brief packaged around the wrong record is worse than none.
2. **Pull the CRM story (read-only):** current properties (owner, lifecycle stage, key custom fields), deal stage + amount + stage history with dates, the last 5-10 logged notes/engagements with dates and authors, open tasks and tickets, and any listed commitments or objections in the notes.
3. **Pull the mail story (read-only):** recent threads with this contact's address - last inbound message (what the customer last said, verbatim key lines), last outbound (what was last promised), and any unanswered inbound flagged prominently.
4. **Reconcile the two sides.** Where mail and CRM disagree (customer says signed, deal stage says negotiating; last note predates three newer emails), state the discrepancy explicitly in the brief rather than silently picking one source.
5. **Assemble the brief:** Who (contact/company, role, relationship age) → Where things stand (stage, amount, open items) → What was last said (both directions, dated) → Commitments & landmines (promises made, objections raised, sensitive history - marked internal) → Suggested next message (purpose-fit opening that acknowledges the last exchange). Every fact carries its source (note date/author or message ID).
6. **Check for an existing artifact before creating one.** Search drafts for a prior brief for this contact and purpose (match on subject convention, e.g. `[Handoff] {contact} - {purpose}`). If found, propose updating/replacing it, not stacking a duplicate.
7. **Present before writing. Stop here.** Show the assembled brief and the intended action ("create draft addressed to X, subject Y") and wait for approval. On approval, create the *draft only*, then verify by re-reading it and return its link. Sending is out of scope - it belongs to the human.

**Re-run behavior.** Idempotent via step 6: re-running refreshes the existing draft (with an "updated {date}" line) instead of creating a second one.

**Fallbacks.** If mail is unavailable, deliver the complete brief inline in chat, formatted to paste into any email client, and say the draft step was skipped. If HubSpot is unavailable, stop - a handoff brief without the CRM story is the cold email this playbook exists to prevent.

## Connector access (required)

This skill cannot run from text alone. It must call live integrations.

1. Confirm each required app is enabled and authenticated in the current AI tool.
2. Discover the exact available connector tools first; do not assume the example names exist.
3. Use read-only calls before proposing writes. For sends, deletes, publishes, refunds, or updates, show a review table and wait for explicit approval.

### Required integrations

- **@hubspot** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `hubspot`.
- **@gmail** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `gmail`.

### If a connector is missing

Stop and tell the user which app is missing. Give setup steps for their tool, then ask them to retry after authentication. Do not substitute generic web search for private workspace data.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- This playbook creates drafts only; it never sends email under any instruction short of the user's separate, explicit send approval.
- Internal note content stays in the internal section of the brief and never enters customer-addressed text.

## Output format

**Subject** - contact/company/deal resolved, with IDs.

**Handoff brief**

- **Who** - contact, role, relationship age, owner history
- **Where things stand** - stage, amount, open tasks/tickets (each with source + date)
- **Last said** - last inbound / last outbound, dated, key lines quoted
- **Commitments & landmines** - promises, objections, sensitive context *(internal)*
- **Discrepancies** - where mail and CRM disagree
- **Suggested next message** - draft body fit to the handoff purpose

**Proposed write** - create/update draft: recipient, subject, existing-draft check result. *(approval checkpoint)*

**Run summary**

| Step | App | Produced | Status |

## Do

- Date and attribute every fact - an undated "customer is unhappy" is gossip, not a brief
- Surface unanswered inbound mail at the top; the handoff recipient inherits that debt first
- State mail↔CRM discrepancies instead of resolving them silently
- Check for an existing brief draft before creating one

## Don't

- Don't send anything - draft creation is this playbook's outer limit
- Don't let internal-note language leak into customer-addressed text
- Don't summarize away the last inbound message; quote its key lines verbatim
- Don't proceed on an ambiguous contact/deal match - list candidates and ask

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The subject record was resolved unambiguously (or candidates were surfaced)
- [ ] Both the CRM story and the mail story were read, with discrepancies stated
- [ ] Every brief fact carries a dated source (note author/date or message ID)
- [ ] The only write was an approved draft, verified after creation, deduped against existing drafts
- [ ] Run summary table shows step, app, produced artifact, status for the whole run
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /automation-connector-playbooks-hubspot-mail-handoff-brief or describe the task from the skill description.

name: automation-connector-playbooks-hubspot-mail-failure-check
description: >
  Diagnose workflow failures for CRM and mail workflows: a connector troubleshooting report. Produces a copy-ready failure report. Triggers: "CRM and mail workflows failure-check", /automation-connector-playbooks-hubspot-mail-failure-check.
metadata:

---
# /automation-connector-playbooks-hubspot-mail-failure-check

Diagnose workflow failures for CRM and mail workflows: a connector troubleshooting report. Produces a copy-ready failure report. Triggers: "CRM and mail workflows failure-check", /automation-connector-playbooks-hubspot-mail-failure-check.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when the mail↔CRM pipeline is supposed to be working but deals keep dying quietly: a customer replied days ago and nobody answered, a contact's logged activity stops while their emails keep arriving, sequences show "sent" to addresses that bounce. This check reads both sides and hunts the *gaps between them* - conversations mail knows about that HubSpot doesn't, commitments HubSpot records that mail can't confirm - and reports each broken handoff with evidence and a proposed repair. It is a diagnosis run: read-only throughout, with repairs listed for a separate approved action.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The check window (default 14 days) and the mail scope: which mailbox/labels represent customer traffic
- Response-time expectation: how long a customer email may sit unanswered before it counts as stalled (default 2 business days)
- Which HubSpot pipelines/deal stages are active-work stages where silence is a symptom (vs closed/parked stages where it isn't)
- Whether logging is expected to be automatic (CRM mail integration on) or manual - this changes what "missing log" means
- Known exclusions: newsletters, automated senders, internal domains to ignore

## Workflow

1. **Read both sides.** From mail: threads in scope within the window, with sender, last-message direction (inbound/outbound), and last-activity date. From HubSpot: contacts and open deals with their last logged activity date, owner, stage, and stage-entry date. All read-only.
2. **Join on email address** (domain → company fallback) to build the pairing: each mail thread mapped to its CRM contact/deal, and each active deal mapped to its recent mail traffic.
3. **Sweep mail→CRM gaps:** inbound customer messages with no reply and no logged CRM activity since (stalled conversation, invisible to the CRM); active senders whose CRM contact shows no logged activity in the window (logging integration silently off or contact unmatched); replies from addresses that don't exist in the CRM at all (conversation happening off the books).
4. **Sweep CRM→mail gaps:** open deals in active stages with zero mail traffic in the window (deal alive on paper only); tasks like "email sent" or sequence steps marked complete with no matching message in the sent mail (phantom completions); logged emails whose recipients have since bounced (dead address still being "worked").
5. **Classify each finding by failure mode:** unanswered customer (needs a human reply), unlogged activity (needs logging repair or contact match fix), phantom completion (needs task/sequence correction), dead address (needs contact data fix), off-books conversation (needs contact creation decision). Severity = customer-facing first, hygiene second.
6. **Attach evidence per finding:** thread subject + message ID + dates on the mail side; object ID, stage, last-activity date on the CRM side. A finding without both sides' evidence is a hypothesis - label it as such.
7. **Propose repairs, don't perform them.** Each finding gets a one-line repair (reply owed by X, log thread Y to contact Z, fix property, create contact) suitable for handing to the approval-queue playbook or a human. This run writes nothing.

**Re-run behavior.** The check is naturally idempotent (read-only). To keep repeat runs comparable, findings carry stable keys (message ID, object ID) so the user can diff this run against the last one and see what was fixed vs what aged.

**Fallbacks.** If HubSpot is unavailable, run the mail-side sweep alone (unanswered inbound, bounce detection) and say plainly that CRM-side gaps are unchecked. If mail is unavailable, run the CRM-side staleness sweep alone with the same caveat. Never present a one-sided run as a full check.

## Connector access (required)

This skill cannot run from text alone. It must call live integrations.

1. Confirm each required app is enabled and authenticated in the current AI tool.
2. Discover the exact available connector tools first; do not assume the example names exist.
3. Use read-only calls before proposing writes. For sends, deletes, publishes, refunds, or updates, show a review table and wait for explicit approval.

### Required integrations

- **@hubspot** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `hubspot`.
- **@gmail** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `gmail`.

### If a connector is missing

Stop and tell the user which app is missing. Give setup steps for their tool, then ask them to retry after authentication. Do not substitute generic web search for private workspace data.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- This playbook is read-only end to end; any repair it proposes executes only through a separately approved action, never inside the check itself.

## Output format

**Scope** - window, mail scope, pipelines checked, thresholds used.

**Failure report**

| # | Failure mode | Who/what (contact, deal, thread) | Evidence (mail side / CRM side) | Age | Severity | Proposed repair |

**Customer-facing first** - the unanswered/stalled items, oldest first, each with the owner who owes the reply.

**Coverage note** - what was checked, what was excluded, and any one-sided sweeps with the reason.

**Run summary**

| Step | App | Produced | Status |

## Do

- Lead with customer-facing failures; hygiene findings come second
- Carry both-sides evidence (message ID + CRM object ID) on every confirmed finding
- Distinguish "no activity logged" from "no activity happened" - check the integration assumption first
- Use stable keys so consecutive runs can be diffed

## Don't

- Don't fix anything mid-check - a failure check that writes is a different, unapproved playbook
- Don't count newsletters, automated senders, or internal mail as stalled conversations
- Don't report a deal as dead solely from CRM silence when its mail thread is active - that is a logging gap, not a dead deal
- Don't present a one-sided sweep (one connector down) as a completed check

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Both directions were swept: mail→CRM gaps and CRM→mail gaps
- [ ] Every confirmed finding has evidence from both apps, or is labeled a hypothesis
- [ ] Findings are classified by failure mode with customer-facing items ranked first
- [ ] No write occurred anywhere in the run
- [ ] Run summary table shows step, app, produced artifact, status for the whole run
```

**Scope** - window, mail scope, pipelines checked, thresholds used.

**Failure report**

| # | Failure mode | Who/what (contact, deal, thread) | Evidence (mail side / CRM side) | Age | Severity | Proposed repair |

**Customer-facing first** - the unanswered/stalled items, oldest first, each with the owner who owes the reply.

**Coverage note** - what was checked, what was excluded, and any one-sided sweeps with the reason.

**Run summary**

| Step | App | Produced | Status |

## Do

- Lead with customer-facing failures; hygiene findings come second
- Carry both-sides evidence (message ID + CRM object ID) on every confirmed finding
- Distinguish "no activity logged" from "no activity happened" - check the integration assumption first
- Use stable keys so consecutive runs can be diffed

## Don't

- Don't fix anything mid-check - a failure check that writes is a different, unapproved playbook
- Don't count newsletters, automated senders, or internal mail as stalled conversations
- Don't report a deal as dead solely from CRM silence when its mail thread is active - that is a logging gap, not a dead deal
- Don't present a one-sided sweep (one connector down) as a completed check

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Both directions were swept: mail→CRM gaps and CRM→mail gaps
- [ ] Every confirmed finding has evidence from both apps, or is labeled a hypothesis
- [ ] Findings are classified by failure mode with customer-facing items ranked first
- [ ] No write occurred anywhere in the run
- [ ] Run summary table shows step, app, produced artifact, status for the whole run

# /automation-connector-playbooks-stripe-sheet-read-only-digest

Summarize live workspace activity for Stripe and spreadsheet workflows: a read-only digest from connected apps. Produces a copy-ready workspace digest. Triggers: "Stripe and spreadsheet workflows read-only-digest", /automation-connector-playbooks-stripe-sheet-read-only-digest.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this as the recurring money briefing: what moved in Stripe this window (charges, refunds, failed payments, disputes, payouts), whether the tracking sheet has kept up, and what needs a decision - all read, nothing written. It answers three questions in one pass: how did we do (window figures from live Stripe reads), is the bookkeeping current (sheet coverage check by Stripe ID), and what's waiting (disputes with due dates, failed payments worth retry attention, payouts in transit). Best fit: a Monday-morning or month-start financial pulse for a founder or bookkeeper.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Digest window (default 7 days; month-to-date for month-start runs)
- The tracking sheet/tab and its Stripe-ID column, so the coverage check can run; if the user has no sheet, run the Stripe-only digest and say the coverage section is skipped
- Which events matter: all money movement, or thresholds (e.g. only charges above X, only failures on subscriptions)
- Known exclusions: test mode, internal transfers, comped customers
- Delivery form: chat only, or also a paste-ready block for notes/standup

## Workflow

1. **Read Stripe (read-only).** Pull the window's charges, refunds, failed payments, disputes, and payouts with IDs, dates, amounts, currencies, statuses, and customer identifiers. All figures verbatim; all aggregates computed only over these fetched records and labeled as such.
2. **Compute the window picture:** gross charged, refunded, net; count of new vs returning customers among the fetched charges where the data allows; payouts paid vs in transit with dates. No extrapolation, no run-rate talk unless asked - this is a report of records, not a forecast.
3. **Read the sheet and check coverage.** Load the tab's Stripe IDs for the window and mark each Stripe object `booked` / `not booked`. The coverage line - "N of M window transactions appear in the sheet" - is the digest's early-warning system for dead automations and skipped bookkeeping weeks. List the unbooked IDs oldest-first.
4. **Build the attention list:** disputes with their response deadlines (deadline first - these expire), failed payments grouped by customer with the failure reason Stripe records, refunds above the user's threshold, and any payout whose status warrants watching. Each item carries its Stripe ID and a link where available.
5. **Note anomalies as observations:** a refund spike relative to the rest of the window's fetched records, repeat failures from one customer, currency oddities - phrased as "observed in this window", never as diagnosis or invented benchmarks.
6. **Close:** a quiet-but-fine line covering what was checked and found unremarkable, then suggested next actions as options (respond to dispute X by date, book the unbooked IDs via the approval-queue playbook, review customer Y's failures) - all explicitly outside this read-only run.

**Re-run behavior.** Naturally idempotent - nothing is written. Items keyed by Stripe ID; when the prior digest is in context, unresolved items are marked `carried over` with age (a dispute carried over twice is louder than a new one).

**Fallbacks.** If the sheet connector is down, deliver the Stripe-only digest and mark the coverage section skipped - never guess at what the sheet contains. If Stripe is down, stop: a money digest without the money system has nothing truthful to say beyond connector-setup guidance.

## Connector access (required)

This skill cannot run from text alone. It must call live integrations.

1. Confirm each required app is enabled and authenticated in the current AI tool.
2. Discover the exact available connector tools first; do not assume the example names exist.
3. Use read-only calls before proposing writes. For sends, deletes, publishes, refunds, or updates, show a review table and wait for explicit approval.

### Required integrations

- **@stripe** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `stripe`.
- **@google-sheets** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `google-sheets`.

### If a connector is missing

Stop and tell the user which app is missing. Give setup steps for their tool, then ask them to retry after authentication. Do not substitute generic web search for private workspace data.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- This playbook writes nothing in either app; every actionable finding is handed off as a suggestion only.
- No forecasts, benchmarks, or industry comparisons - the digest reports fetched records and labels every aggregate as a sum over them.

## Output format

**Scope** - window, thresholds, exclusions, sheet checked (or skipped).

**Window figures** - gross / refunds / net / payouts, labeled as sums over fetched records.

**Coverage check** - N of M booked; unbooked IDs oldest-first: | Stripe ID | Date | Amount | Type |

**Attention list** - | Item | Stripe ID | Amount | Deadline/age | Why it needs eyes |

**Observations** - window-internal anomalies, phrased as observations.

**Quiet-but-fine** - one line confirming the healthy remainder.

**Suggested next actions** - options only; nothing executed.

**Run summary**

| Step | App | Produced | Status |

## Do

- Lead the attention list with dispute deadlines - they expire, everything else waits
- Label every aggregate as computed over fetched records for the stated window
- Run the coverage check by Stripe ID, not by comparing totals - totals can agree by accident
- Carry over unresolved items with their age so recurring neglect is visible

## Don't

- Don't write anything - no sheet appends, no Stripe actions, even if asked mid-run (route to the approval-queue playbook)
- Don't extrapolate revenue, project month-end, or cite industry norms
- Don't report the coverage check as passed when the sheet lacks a Stripe-ID column - say the check was weak-keyed
- Don't bury a dispute deadline below aesthetic revenue numbers

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] All figures trace to Stripe fields; aggregates are labeled fetched-record sums
- [ ] Coverage check ran by Stripe ID (or its absence was declared) with unbooked IDs listed
- [ ] Dispute deadlines lead the attention list
- [ ] Zero writes occurred; next actions are phrased as options
- [ ] Run summary table shows step, app, produced artifact, status for the whole run
```

---
name: automation-connector-playbooks-stripe-sheet-approval-queue
description: >
  Prepare changes for approval for Stripe and spreadsheet workflows: a safe review queue before any write action. Produces a copy-ready approval queue. Triggers: "Stripe and spreadsheet workflows approval-queue", /automation-connector-playbooks-stripe-sheet-approval-queue.
