---
# /automation-connector-playbooks-zendesk-slack-read-only-digest

Summarize live workspace activity for support and Slack workflows: a read-only digest from connected apps. Produces a copy-ready workspace digest. Triggers: "support and Slack workflows read-only-digest", /automation-connector-playbooks-zendesk-slack-read-only-digest.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this as the support lead's shift-start or Monday briefing: the ticket queue's live state (oldest waiting customers, priority breakdown, breach-risk items), what the escalation channel discussed, and - the join that makes it one report instead of two - which escalated tickets moved and which discussions never produced ticket action. Reads both apps, writes to neither: no posts, no ticket changes. Best fit: a recurring "state of the queue and the escalation channel" pulse that takes two minutes to read and replaces twenty minutes of tab-hopping.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Digest window (default: since yesterday for daily runs; 7 days for weekly)
- Queue scope: which views/groups/brands count, and whether only unsolved tickets or also recently solved ones
- The escalation channel(s) and the ticket-ID format used there (the join key)
- Attention thresholds: customer-waiting age (default 2 business days), and what counts as high-priority for the top of the report
- Delivery form: chat only, or also a paste-ready block for the standup notes

## Workflow

1. **Read the queue.** Tickets in scope: ID, subject, requester org, priority, status, created/updated, assignee, and the age of the oldest unanswered customer message. Compute queue counts by status and priority as sums over the fetched set, labeled as such.
2. **Read the escalation channel.** Window's messages: extract ticket IDs (standard format; loose matches marked weak), capture per referenced ticket the latest post, author, permalink, and any resolution-sounding thread language.
3. **Join on ticket ID.** Every escalated ticket carries both sides: channel activity + live ticket state. Everything downstream uses this joined view.
4. **Build the waiting-customers list:** unsolved tickets past the waiting threshold, oldest first, each showing whether it has been escalated (with permalink) or is waiting in silence - the silent ones rank above the escalated ones at equal age, because nobody is watching them.
5. **Build the escalation-channel review:** tickets discussed this window with what changed on the ticket since the discussion (status/assignee/comment movement, from live fields) - giving the lead an instant "the channel worked / the channel was shouting into the void" read per item. Mismatches (resolution language vs open ticket) are noted as observations for the failure-check playbook, not diagnosed here.
6. **Build the movement section:** tickets solved or downgraded in the window, and new high-priority arrivals, so the lead sees flow, not just backlog.
7. **Close:** a quiet-but-fine line covering what was checked and found orderly, then suggested next actions as options (nudge thread X, reply to ticket Y, run the escalation sweep via approval-queue) - all explicitly outside this read-only run. Every line carries ticket ID and/or permalink.

**Re-run behavior.** Naturally idempotent - nothing is written. Lines keyed by ticket ID; with the prior digest in context, persisting items are marked `carried over` with accumulated age, so a customer waiting across three digests reads as three digests old.

**Fallbacks.** If Slack is unavailable, deliver the queue-side digest with a banner that channel context is unchecked. If Zendesk is unavailable, deliver the channel-side review noting ticket states are unverified. Never present a half-digest as whole.

## Connector access (required)

This skill cannot run from text alone. It must call live integrations.

1. Confirm each required app is enabled and authenticated in the current AI tool.
2. Discover the exact available connector tools first; do not assume the example names exist.
3. Use read-only calls before proposing writes. For sends, deletes, publishes, refunds, or updates, show a review table and wait for explicit approval.

### Required integrations

- **@zendesk** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `zendesk`.
- **@slack** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `slack`.

### If a connector is missing

Stop and tell the user which app is missing. Give setup steps for their tool, then ask them to retry after authentication. Do not substitute generic web search for private workspace data.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- This playbook writes to neither app under any circumstances; every actionable finding is handed off as a suggestion only.
- The digest reports field-level facts and timestamps, never agent-performance judgments.

## Output format

**Scope** - window, queue views, channels, thresholds.

**Queue counts** - by status and priority, labeled as sums over fetched tickets.

**Waiting customers** - | Ticket ID | Requester org | Waiting since | Priority | Escalated? (permalink / silent) |

**Escalation-channel review** - | Ticket ID | Last channel post (permalink, time) | Ticket movement since | Note |

**Movement** - solved/downgraded and new high-priority arrivals this window.

**Observations** - resolution-language mismatches flagged for the failure-check playbook.

**Quiet-but-fine** - one line confirming the healthy remainder.

**Suggested next actions** - options only; nothing executed.

**Run summary**

| Step | App | Produced | Status |

## Do

- Join channel discussion to live ticket state on ticket ID - that join is the report's value
- Rank silently-waiting customers above escalated ones at equal age
- Mark carried-over items with accumulated age across digests
- Label all counts as sums over the fetched scope

## Don't

- Don't write anything - no posts, no ticket updates, even if asked mid-run (route to the approval-queue playbook)
- Don't grade agents or attribute delays to people; report tickets and timestamps
- Don't treat Slack resolution language as ticket truth - the status field is the record
- Don't present a half-digest as complete when one connector was down

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Queue and channel were both read and joined on ticket ID with weak matches marked
- [ ] Waiting list ranks oldest and silent items first, with escalation state shown per row
- [ ] All counts trace to fetched records; every line carries an ID or permalink
- [ ] Zero writes occurred; next actions are phrased as options
- [ ] Run summary table shows step, app, produced artifact, status for the whole run
```

Create the folder if needed, then write SKILL.md with this exact content:

```markdown
---
name: automation-connector-playbooks-zendesk-slack-approval-queue
description: >
  Prepare changes for approval for support and Slack workflows: a safe review queue before any write action. Produces a copy-ready approval queue. Triggers: "support and Slack workflows approval-queue", /automation-connector-playbooks-zendesk-slack-approval-queue.
metadata:
  category: automation
  type: connector
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand

---
# /automation-connector-playbooks-zendesk-slack-approval-queue

Prepare changes for approval for support and Slack workflows: a safe review queue before any write action. Produces a copy-ready approval queue. Triggers: "support and Slack workflows approval-queue", /automation-connector-playbooks-zendesk-slack-approval-queue.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when tickets that qualify for escalation need to reach the team's Slack escalation channel, and the team has been burned both ways: escalations nobody posted, and the same ticket posted three times by three people. The playbook reads the ticket queue against the escalation rules, drafts one Slack post per qualifying ticket, checks channel history by ticket ID so already-escalated tickets are skipped, and holds every post in an approval queue. Nothing lands in the channel until specific rows are approved; Zendesk itself is untouched. Best fit: the shift-start escalation sweep run by a support lead.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Escalation criteria: which ticket states qualify (e.g. priority urgent/high, pending > N hours, breach-risk flags, VIP requester tags) - get the team's actual rule, don't assume one
- The destination channel(s), and whether different ticket classes route to different channels
- The post template the team expects: which fields (ticket ID, requester, subject, age, priority, assignee, last-message direction) and the mention policy - who may be @-mentioned, if anyone (default: no individual mentions, no @here/@channel)
- The dedupe convention: ticket ID in message text is the standard key; confirm the ID format used in this channel (e.g. `#4521` vs `ZD-4521`)
- Lookback window for both the ticket sweep and the channel-history dedupe search (default: tickets updated in 7 days; channel history 14 days)

## Workflow

1. **Read Zendesk (no writes).** Pull tickets matching the escalation criteria: ticket ID, subject, requester and organization, priority, status, created/updated times, assignee, and the age of the oldest unanswered customer message. Facts from live fields only.
2. **Read Slack channel history (still read-only).** Search the destination channel(s) for each qualifying ticket's ID in the agreed format. A hit - by any author, human or bot - turns that ticket's row into `skip (already escalated)` with the existing message link. This search is the whole difference between a useful sweep and channel spam.
3. **Draft one post per remaining ticket** from the template: ticket ID (in the channel's standard format so future dedupes find it), subject, requester org, priority, the specific reason it qualifies (quoting the rule it tripped: "pending 26h > 24h rule"), and the ticket link. ```markdown
---
name: automation-connector-playbooks-zendesk-slack-handoff-brief
description: >
  Create a cross-app handoff for support and Slack workflows: a handoff package across tools and teams. Produces a copy-ready handoff brief. Triggers: "support and Slack workflows handoff-brief", /automation-connector-playbooks-zendesk-slack-handoff-brief.
metadata:
  category: automation
  type: connector
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: medium
  real_world_use_case: Live Connectors & Automations
  connectors: zendesk,slack
  integrations: @zendesk,@slack
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /automation-connector-playbooks-zendesk-slack-handoff-brief

Create a cross-app handoff for support and Slack workflows: a handoff package across tools and teams. Produces a copy-ready handoff brief. Triggers: "support and Slack workflows handoff-brief", /automation-connector-playbooks-zendesk-slack-handoff-brief.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when one ticket needs to move into Slack with its whole story attached: an escalation to engineering that needs reproduction steps and environment details, a VIP complaint the account owner must see in full, or a shift handover where the next person should not have to read forty ticket events to catch up. A bare link makes the reader do the work; a paraphrased summary loses the one detail that mattered. This playbook reads the complete ticket - every public reply, internal note, and field change - distills it into a structured brief with the customer's own words preserved where they carry the signal, and posts it to the chosen channel only after approval. Zendesk is read-only throughout.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The ticket: ID or a description precise enough to resolve to one ("the Acme billing escalation" → confirm the ID before pulling)
- The audience and purpose: engineering escalation, account-owner alert, shift handover - this decides which details lead (repro steps vs relationship history vs open loops)
- Destination: channel or DM, and whether the brief should post as one message or a message with the detail in its thread
- Sensitivity rules: internal notes may contain candid agent commentary - default is to summarize their substance without quoting agents verbatim into a wider channel; confirm the rule
- The dedupe convention: ticket ID in message text (confirm the channel's format), so re-runs and other tools can find this brief

## Workflow

1. **Resolve the ticket.** Confirm ID and subject from a live read; ambiguity → list candidates and ask.
2. **Pull the full ticket (read-only):** requester and organization, status/priority/assignee, created and key timestamps, every public message in order, internal notes, and field-change events that matter to the story (priority raises, reassignments, escalation tags). Note SLA/breach data where the connector exposes it.
3. **Distill the arc, don't transcribe it:** the issue in one line, what has been tried (each attempt with its outcome and date), what the customer said at the emotional turning points - quoted verbatim, briefly - and the current blocking question. A forty-event ticket usually reduces to five load-bearing facts; find them and cite each to its event.
4. **Shape by audience.** Engineering: environment/version details, exact error text verbatim, reproduction sequence, what support already ruled out. Account owner: relationship stakes, tone trajectory, promises already made. Handover: open loops, who owes what next, the deadline landscape. Apply the sensitivity rule to internal-note content for the destination's audience width.
5. **Compose the Slack message(s):** headline block (ticket ID in standard format, subject, requester org, priority, age, current owner, one-line status), then the distilled brief, then the ticket link and explicit next-action-with-owner. If threading was chosen, the headline is the message and the detail goes in its thread.
6. **Dedupe before posting.** Search the destination's history for this ticket ID with a brief-style post. Found → propose replying in that thread with an update instead of a fresh top-level duplicate.
7. **Present, then post on approval. Stop first.** Show the full message text and destination; wait for explicit approval. On approval, post, verify by re-reading from history, and return the permalink. Mentions follow the user's stated policy only.

**Re-run behavior.** Idempotent via step 6 - re-runs extend the existing brief's thread with a dated update rather than reposting; the ticket ID in the message text is what makes that discoverable.

**Fallbacks.** If Slack is unavailable, deliver the finished brief inline, formatted for pasting, with the note that dedupe against channel history could not run. If Zendesk is unavailable, stop - a handoff brief invented from memory of a ticket is exactly the misinformation an escalation channel cannot absorb.

## Connector access (required)

This skill cannot run from text alone. It must call live integrations.

1. Confirm each required app is enabled and authenticated in the current AI tool.
2. Discover the exact available connector tools first; do not assume the example names exist.
3. Use read-only calls before proposing writes. For sends, deletes, publishes, refunds, or updates, show a review table and wait for explicit approval.

### Required integrations

- **@zendesk** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `zendesk`.
- **@slack** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `slack`.

### If a connector is missing

Stop and tell the user which app is missing. Give setup steps for their tool, then ask them to retry after authentication. Do not substitute generic web search for private workspace data.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- Zendesk is strictly read-only; the approved Slack post is the only write this playbook makes.
- Internal-note content is filtered per the sensitivity rule before it can reach a wider channel; agent commentary is never quoted verbatim without explicit approval.

## Output format

**Ticket** - resolved ID, subject, requester org, link.

**Proposed Slack post**

- **Headline block** - ticket ID, priority, age, owner, one-line status
- **The story** - issue, attempts with outcomes (dated), customer's key words (verbatim, brief)
- **Audience section** - repro/environment (engineering) or stakes/promises (account) or open loops (handover)
- **Next action** - what, who, by when, plus the ticket link

**Destination & dedupe** - channel/DM, threading choice, existing-brief search result. *(approval checkpoint)*

**Posted** (after approval) - permalink, verified.

**Run summary**

| Step | App | Produced | Status |

## Do

- Quote error text and the customer's pivotal lines verbatim; paraphrase kills the diagnostic detail
- Cite each distilled fact to its ticket event (date/author) so the reader can verify
- End with a named next action and owner - a brief without one is trivia
- Search channel history for the ticket ID before posting; update the existing thread when found

## Don't

- Don't modify the ticket - no status, assignee, tag, or comment changes
- Don't leak candid internal-note commentary verbatim into a wide channel against the sensitivity rule
- Don't post a wall transcript of the ticket; distillation with citations is the deliverable
- Don't @-mention anyone outside the user's stated mention policy

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The ticket was resolved unambiguously and read in full, notes and events included
- [ ] Every distilled fact cites its source event; key quotes are verbatim
- [ ] The sensitivity rule was applied to internal-note content for this destination
- [ ] The only write was the approved, verified Slack post, deduped against channel history
- [ ] Run summary table shows step, app, produced artifact, status for the whole run
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /automation-connector-playbooks-zendesk-slack-handoff-brief or describe the task from the skill description.

# /automation-connector-playbooks-zendesk-slack-failure-check

Diagnose workflow failures for support and Slack workflows: a connector troubleshooting report. Produces a copy-ready failure report. Triggers: "support and Slack workflows failure-check", /automation-connector-playbooks-zendesk-slack-failure-check.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when the escalation loop between the ticket queue and the Slack channel has holes and customers are falling through them: a ticket was escalated in Slack days ago and hasn't been touched since, an urgent ticket aged past the escalation rule and never appeared in the channel, or a Slack thread ends with "fixed, closing it out" while the ticket still sits open and the customer still waits. This check reads both sides, matches escalation posts to tickets by ticket ID, and reports every broken handoff in the escalate→act→resolve loop - read-only, evidence-paired, with proposed repairs handed off rather than performed.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The escalation channel(s) and the ticket-ID format used in posts (e.g. `#4521`, `ZD-4521`) - the match key for everything
- The escalation criteria the team uses (priority, pending-hours, breach flags), so never-escalated qualifying tickets can be detected
- Action expectation: how long after a Slack escalation a ticket update is expected (default 4 business hours)
- The check window (default 7 days of channel history and tickets updated in 14 days)
- Known exceptions: tickets legitimately parked (waiting on engineering fix with linked issue), channels or bots to ignore

## Workflow

1. **Read the Slack side.** Escalation-channel messages in the window; extract ticket IDs from each (standard format first, loose patterns second - loose matches marked weak). Capture per post: timestamp, author, permalink, and whether its thread contains resolution-sounding language ("fixed", "resolved", "closing").
2. **Read the Zendesk side.** Tickets referenced by those IDs: status, priority, assignee, last-update time, last-comment direction (public reply vs internal note). Separately, pull tickets matching the escalation criteria in the window whether or not they appear in Slack.
3. **Match and verify IDs.** Slack-posted IDs that match no ticket become their own finding class (`dangling reference` - typo or deleted ticket), not silent drops.
4. **Sweep escalated-then-ignored:** posts whose ticket shows no update (no comment, no status/assignee change) since the post plus the action window. Rank by post age; these are the loudest failures - the team said "look at this" and nobody did.
5. **Sweep qualified-but-never-escalated:** tickets tripping the escalation criteria with no matching post in channel history - the silent counterpart, invisible until a breach.
6. **Sweep resolution mismatches:** threads with resolution language whose ticket remains open/pending (work done, ticket forgotten - the customer keeps waiting for a closure that happened only in Slack), and the inverse: solved tickets whose escalation thread was never answered (harmless-looking, but it trains the channel to be ignored - report as low severity).
7. **Classify, rank, and propose repairs.** Severity order: customer-still-waiting items first (ignored escalations, phantom resolutions), silent non-escalations second, hygiene (dangling references, unanswered threads) third. Each finding pairs Slack evidence (permalink, timestamp) with Zendesk evidence (ticket ID, status, last-update) or is labeled a hypothesis. Repairs - nudge the thread, update/close the ticket, escalate ticket X - are proposals for the approval-queue playbook or a human. This run writes nothing.

**Re-run behavior.** Read-only and naturally idempotent. Findings keyed by ticket ID + failure mode so consecutive runs diff cleanly; carried-over findings show accumulated age.

**Fallbacks.** If Slack is unavailable, run only the qualified-but-never-escalated sweep from Zendesk criteria and say the channel side is unchecked. If Zendesk is unavailable, list the window's escalation posts with extracted IDs and say ticket verification is blind. Never present a one-sided sweep as the full check.

## Connector access (required)

This skill cannot run from text alone. It must call live integrations.

1. Confirm each required app is enabled and authenticated in the current AI tool.
2. Discover the exact available connector tools first; do not assume the example names exist.
3. Use read-only calls before proposing writes. For sends, deletes, publishes, refunds, or updates, show a review table and wait for explicit approval.

### Required integrations

- **@zendesk** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `zendesk`.
- **@slack** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `slack`.

### If a connector is missing

Stop and tell the user which app is missing. Give setup steps for their tool, then ask them to retry after authentication. Do not substitute generic web search for private workspace data.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- This playbook is read-only in both apps end to end; every repair executes only through a separately approved run.
- Findings name tickets and threads, not culprits - the report states field-level facts and timestamps, never fault.

## Output format

**Scope** - channels, ID format, criteria, action window, exceptions honored.

**Failure report**

| # | Failure mode (ignored escalation / never escalated / phantom resolution / dangling ref / unanswered thread) | Ticket ID | Slack evidence (permalink, time) | Zendesk evidence (status, last update) | Age | Severity | Proposed repair |

**Customer-still-waiting list** - ignored escalations and phantom resolutions, oldest first.

**Silent non-escalations** - qualifying tickets never posted, with the rule each tripped.

**Coverage note** - weak-key matches counted, exceptions excluded, any one-sided sweep declared.

**Run summary**

| Step | App | Produced | Status |

## Do

- Match on ticket IDs in the channel's standard format and mark loose-pattern matches weak
- Distinguish public replies from internal notes when judging "no action since escalation"
- Rank customer-still-waiting failures above channel-hygiene findings
- Show carried-over age on findings that persist across runs

## Don't

- Don't post nudges, close tickets, or fix anything mid-check - diagnosis and repair are separate approvals
- Don't count a bot acknowledgment or emoji reaction as action on an escalation
- Don't treat resolution language in Slack as ground truth - the ticket's status field is the record
- Don't present a one-sided sweep (one connector down) as a completed check

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] All three loop directions were swept: escalated-then-ignored, qualified-but-never-escalated, resolution mismatches
- [ ] Every finding pairs Slack permalink evidence with Zendesk field evidence, or is labeled a hypothesis
- [ ] Severity ranks customer-still-waiting items first
- [ ] Zero writes occurred in either app
- [ ] Run summary table shows step, app, produced artifact, status for the whole run
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /automation-connector-playbooks-zendesk-slack-failure-check or describe the task from the skill description.

Install the skill 'automation-connector-playbooks-figma-linear-read-only-digest' by writing the file below.

Universal path: ./skills/automation-connector-playbooks-figma-linear-read-only-digest/SKILL.md
Grok path: ~/.grok/skills/automation-connector-playbooks-figma-linear-read-only-digest/SKILL.md

Create the folder if needed, then write SKILL.md with this exact content:

```markdown
---
name: automation-connector-playbooks-figma-linear-read-only-digest
description: >
  Summarize live workspace activity for design and issue workflows: a read-only digest from connected apps. Produces a copy-ready workspace digest. Triggers: "design and issue workflows read-only-digest", /automation-connector-playbooks-figma-linear-read-only-digest.
metadata:
  category: automation
  type: connector
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: medium
  real_world_use_case: Live Connectors & Automations
  connectors: figma,linear
  integrations: @figma,@linear
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /automation-connector-playbooks-figma-linear-read-only-digest

Summarize live workspace activity for design and issue workflows: a read-only digest from connected apps. Produces a copy-ready workspace digest. Triggers: "design and issue workflows read-only-digest", /automation-connector-playbooks-figma-linear-read-only-digest.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this before design review or sprint planning, when the question is "where does design feedback actually stand?" Figma holds the comments; Linear holds the work items; the gap between them is where feedback dies. This digest reads both sides and shows: which Figma comments have a tracking issue, which are unresolved and untracked, which Linear design issues point at files whose frames have since changed, and which comment threads resolved without their issue closing. Read-only - it files nothing.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Figma scope: which files or project (by name/link) - not the whole team space
- Linear scope: team key and, if used, the label/project that marks design-sourced issues (e.g. `design-feedback`)
- Window: default comments and issue activity from the last 14 days
- Linking convention: does the team paste Figma links into issues, prefix issues with the file name, or nothing consistent (affects match confidence)
- Caps: max comments and issues to inspect before disclosing truncation

## Workflow

**Phase 1 - Read Figma.** Pull comment threads in scoped files within the window: comment ID, file and node (frame) reference, author, text, resolved/unresolved state, last reply time.

**Phase 2 - Read Linear.** Pull scoped issues: identifier, title, state, assignee, labels, and any Figma URLs in the description or comments. Also pull recently completed issues that carry Figma links, for the closed-side check.

**Phase 3 - Cross-match.** Strongest key: a Figma node URL inside a Linear issue matches that comment's file+node. Next: comment text quoted in the issue body. Weakest: title similarity - mark as guess. Then bucket: tracked-and-open (healthy), unresolved-untracked (feedback with no issue - the real backlog leak), resolved-in-figma-but-issue-open (probably done, close candidate), and issue-closed-but-comment-unresolved (someone should resolve or the feedback recurs).

**Phase 4 - Compose the digest.** Group by Figma file, then by frame. Every row links both artifacts. No writes: no comments resolved, no issues created or transitioned.

**Re-run behavior.** Idempotent - reads only. Dedupe rows on comment ID and issue identifier so overlapping windows do not double-report. State the as-of time; design files mutate mid-meeting.

**Fallbacks.** If Linear is unreachable, deliver the Figma-side list of unresolved threads (that alone is useful triage) and mark tracking state unknown. If Figma is unreachable, list Linear's design-labeled issues with their embedded links and say the design-side state is unverified.

## Connector access (required)

This skill cannot run from text alone. It must call live integrations.

1. Confirm each required app is enabled and authenticated in the current AI tool.
2. Discover the exact available connector tools first; do not assume the example names exist.
3. Use read-only calls before proposing writes. For sends, deletes, publishes, refunds, or updates, show a review table and wait for explicit approval.

### Required integrations

- **@figma** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `figma`.
- **@linear** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `linear`.

### If a connector is missing

Stop and tell the user which app is missing. Give setup steps for their tool, then ask them to retry after authentication. Do not substitute generic web search for private workspace data.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- This playbook is read-only end to end. "Just create the missing issues" is the approval-queue playbook's job, behind its own review table.

## Output format

**Scope & window** - files, team, labels, dates, as-of timestamp.

**Feedback tracking digest**

| Figma file / frame | Comment (author, age) | Linear issue | Bucket | Match confidence | Links |

**Leaks** - unresolved-untracked comments first (oldest first), then close-candidates, then closed-issue/unresolved-comment mismatches.

**Run summary**

| Step | App | Produced | Status |

## Do

- Anchor matches on file+node URLs; they are the only stable cross-app key this pair has
- Show comment age - a three-week-old unresolved comment is the headline
- Mark title-similarity matches as guesses so nobody closes an issue off a wrong link
- Disclose truncation and which files were not swept

## Don't

- Don't treat a resolved Figma thread as shipped work - resolution often means "discussed", not "done"
- Don't match on designer display names across apps; the same person is often named differently in each
- Don't collapse multiple comments on one frame into one row if they track to different issues
- Don't resolve, react, comment, or transition anything - one write breaks the digest's re-run guarantee

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every row links both the Figma node and the Linear issue (or explicitly says untracked)
- [ ] Buckets cover all four states, including the two mismatch directions
- [ ] Match confidence is labeled per row; guesses are visible
- [ ] Zero write calls to either app
- [ ] Run summary shows step, app, produced, status
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /automation-connector-playbooks-figma-linear-read-only-digest or describe the task from the skill description.

# /automation-connector-playbooks-figma-linear-approval-queue

Prepare changes for approval for design and issue workflows: a safe review queue before any write action. Produces a copy-ready approval queue. Triggers: "design and issue workflows approval-queue", /automation-connector-playbooks-figma-linear-approval-queue.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this after a design review lands a pile of Figma comments and someone must turn them into Linear issues without creating duplicates or losing nuance. It reads unresolved comment threads, drafts one issue per actionable thread (with the file+node link embedded as the dedupe key), checks Linear for issues already pointing at those nodes, and queues everything for approval before a single issue is created. Also handles the reverse tidy-up: proposing state changes for issues whose Figma threads have since resolved.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Figma scope: file(s) or project, and whether to take all unresolved threads or only those since a date / since last run
- Linear target: team key, project/cycle to file into, default label (e.g. `design-feedback`), default priority and assignee rules (assign to frame owner? leave unassigned?)
- What counts as actionable: skip pure questions and praise, or queue them as issues too?
- Title convention: e.g. `[FileName] Frame - short imperative` - confirm or infer from existing issues
- Whether to also queue close-candidates (issue open, thread resolved)

## Workflow

**Phase 1 - Read Figma (no writes).** Collect unresolved comment threads in scope: comment ID, file+node URL, author, full thread text (root and replies - replies often contain the actual decision), and thread age.

**Phase 2 - Dedupe against Linear (still read-only).** For each thread, search Linear for issues containing its file+node URL. Found and open → `skip (already tracked)` linking the issue. Found but done → flag as `reopened feedback?` for human judgment, since re-filing may be correct. Nothing found → candidate.

**Phase 3 - Draft and queue. Stop here.** For each candidate draft the issue: title per convention, description containing the quoted comment thread, the Figma node link on its own line (this is the dedupe key for future runs - never omit it), proposed label, priority, project. Close-candidates appear as `mark Done?` rows with evidence. Present the full queue and wait for explicit approval by row or `apply all`. No issue is created or transitioned before that.

**Phase 4 - Execute approved rows.** Create issues one by one; after each, re-read it to confirm the node link survived formatting. Report created identifiers. Do not resolve Figma threads automatically - instead propose a short Figma reply ("Tracked as ABC-123") as an optional second queue, since writing into the design file is a separate blast radius.

**Re-run behavior.** Idempotent through the node-URL key: re-running after a partial failure re-skips everything already created, including issues created manually in between, as long as they carry the link. Threads with no node link fall back to quoted-text search - lower confidence, say so.

**Fallbacks.** If Linear is unreachable, deliver the fully drafted issues inline (title, body, label, priority) ready to paste, each with its node link. If Figma is unreachable, stop - drafting issues from remembered feedback invents requirements.

## Connector access (required)

This skill cannot run from text alone. It must call live integrations.

1. Confirm each required app is enabled and authenticated in the current AI tool.
2. Discover the exact available connector tools first; do not assume the example names exist.
3. Use read-only calls before proposing writes. For sends, deletes, publishes, refunds, or updates, show a review table and wait for explicit approval.

### Required integrations

- **@figma** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `figma`.
- **@linear** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `linear`.

### If a connector is missing

Stop and tell the user which app is missing. Give setup steps for their tool, then ask them to retry after authentication. Do not substitute generic web search for private workspace data.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- Issue creation in Linear and replies/resolution in Figma are separate approval queues - approving one never implies the other.
- Never resolve a designer's comment thread on their behalf without an explicit per-thread approval.

## Output format

**Scope** - files, team/project, actionability rule, title convention.

**Approval queue**

| # | Action | Figma thread (frame, author, age) | Proposed Linear issue (title, label, priority) | Duplicate check | Notes |

**- approval checkpoint - nothing below runs until approved -**

**Execution report** (after approval)

| # | Created / transitioned | Identifier + link | Node link verified | Status |

**Run summary**

| Step | App | Produced | Status |

## Do

- Quote the whole thread in the issue body - the decision usually lives in reply #3, not the root comment
- Embed the file+node URL verbatim; it is the only key that makes every future run dedupe correctly
- Keep `skip (already tracked)` rows visible with their issue links
- Verify the node link survived issue creation before reporting success

## Don't

- Don't merge several comments on one frame into one issue unless they share one fix - splitting later loses the thread linkage
- Don't create issues for resolved threads just because they matched the date window
- Don't auto-assign by Figma display name; map to Linear users by confirmed identity or leave unassigned
- Don't re-run after a partial failure without the Phase-2 dedupe pass - that is exactly when duplicates get minted

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every candidate issue carries the Figma node URL as its dedupe key
- [ ] Linear was searched for that key before every create
- [ ] Full thread text (with replies) is quoted, not summarized into ambiguity
- [ ] Figma-side writes queued separately from Linear-side writes
- [ ] Run summary shows step, app, produced, status
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /automation-connector-playbooks-figma-linear-approval-queue or describe the task from the skill description.

# /automation-connector-playbooks-figma-linear-handoff-brief

Create a cross-app handoff for design and issue workflows: a handoff package across tools and teams. Produces a copy-ready handoff brief. Triggers: "design and issue workflows handoff-brief", /automation-connector-playbooks-figma-linear-handoff-brief.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this at the design-to-engineering boundary: a feature's design is "ready for build" and engineering needs one brief that says which Figma frames are final, which are still contested (live unresolved threads), and how the Linear issues map onto them. Also fits designer offboarding or a design-ownership swap. The failure it prevents: engineers build from a frame that still had an open "this flow is wrong" thread, or a new designer inherits files with no idea which feedback is settled.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Occasion: build handoff, designer offboarding, or ownership swap - the brief's emphasis differs
- Figma scope: the exact file(s)/pages covering the feature or the departing designer's work
- Linear scope: team + project/cycle holding the corresponding issues
- Who receives the brief (eng lead, incoming designer) and what they already know
- Freshness bar: how recent must a frame's last edit be to call it current (default: note anything untouched >30 days)
- Where the brief lives: inline only, or posted as a Linear document/issue (a write, requiring approval)

## Workflow

**Phase 1 - Read Figma.** Per scoped page/frame: last-modified time, unresolved comment threads (author, age, text), and resolved-thread density (a frame with 15 resolved threads had churn worth mentioning). Identify frames referenced by prototypes or marked ready, per the team's convention if one exists.

**Phase 2 - Read Linear.** Pull the scoped issues: state, assignee, estimates if used, and embedded Figma links. Build the frame↔issue map from those links; issues with no Figma link and frames with no issue both matter.

**Phase 3 - Classify and cross-check.** Per frame: `settled` (no unresolved threads, tracked, recently touched), `contested` (unresolved threads - quote them), `stale` (untouched past the freshness bar but still referenced by open issues), `untracked` (real design, no issue). Per issue: `well-anchored` (links a live frame) or `dangling` (links nothing, or a deleted/moved node - these silently break builds).

**Phase 4 - Compose the brief, then checkpoint.** Deliver inline in the format below. If posting to Linear was requested: search first for an existing brief document/issue from a prior run (match on title convention) and propose update-in-place over duplicate creation; show the one-row review table and wait for approval.

**Re-run behavior.** Reads idempotent; the existing-brief check prevents a trail of near-identical handoff docs. Re-runs highlight what changed since the stamped as-of time when the prior brief is available.

**Fallbacks.** If Linear is unreachable, ship the design-state half (settled/contested/stale per frame) and mark tracking unknown. If Figma is unreachable, do not ship - a design handoff that cannot see the designs is the issue list cosplaying as a brief; say so.

## Connector access (required)

This skill cannot run from text alone. It must call live integrations.

1. Confirm each required app is enabled and authenticated in the current AI tool.
2. Discover the exact available connector tools first; do not assume the example names exist.
3. Use read-only calls before proposing writes. For sends, deletes, publishes, refunds, or updates, show a review table and wait for explicit approval.

### Required integrations

- **@figma** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `figma`.
- **@linear** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `linear`.

### If a connector is missing

Stop and tell the user which app is missing. Give setup steps for their tool, then ask them to retry after authentication. Do not substitute generic web search for private workspace data.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- Posting the brief into Linear is a write behind its own approval; this playbook never edits Figma files, resolves threads, or changes issue states as part of composing a brief.

## Output format

**Handoff brief**

- **Context** - feature/designer, sender, recipient, as-of timestamp
- **Frame status map** - table: | Page / frame | Status (settled/contested/stale/untracked) | Open threads (quoted, aged) | Linked issues | Links |
- **Dangling issues** - issues whose Figma anchors are missing or dead, each needing re-anchoring
- **Churn notes** - frames with heavy resolved-thread history worth a verbal walkthrough
- **Start-here list** - for the recipient, ordered: what to build/read first, what to not touch until threads settle

**Proposed write** (only if posting): | Action | Target | Create or update | Status |

**Run summary**

| Step | App | Produced | Status |

## Do

- Quote contested threads verbatim with author and age - the recipient must see the actual objection
- Treat dead node links in issues as findings, not noise; they break engineers mid-sprint
- Note resolved-thread churn on frames that look pristine today
- Stamp the as-of time; frames edited an hour later invalidate rows silently

## Don't

- Don't call a frame "final" from visual polish - only thread state and tracking earn `settled`
- Don't hide untracked frames because no issue exists; that absence is the point
- Don't resolve threads or relabel issues while composing - the brief observes, the queues change
- Don't create a second handoff doc when a prior run's exists; update it

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every scoped frame has a status and every status has evidence links
- [ ] Contested threads are quoted with age, not paraphrased
- [ ] Dangling issues and untracked frames both listed - both directions checked
- [ ] Any Linear post proposed with an existing-brief duplicate check first
- [ ] Run summary shows step, app, produced, status
```

# /automation-connector-playbooks-figma-linear-failure-check

Diagnose workflow failures for design and issue workflows: a connector troubleshooting report. Produces a copy-ready failure report. Triggers: "design and issue workflows failure-check", /automation-connector-playbooks-figma-linear-failure-check.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when the Figma→Linear feedback pipeline (a comment-to-issue automation, a review-day batch process, or a manual convention that "everyone follows") is producing wrong output: duplicate issues for one comment thread, comments that never become issues, issues pointing at nodes that no longer exist, or issue titles referencing the wrong frame. Design files are unusually hostile to sync - nodes get moved, pages renamed, files duplicated for exploration - so this playbook checks the design-side causes as hard as the connector-side ones.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Symptom in one sentence and when it last worked
- How comments are supposed to become issues (tool, batch run, or human convention) and what marks a comment as processed
- One failing example: a specific comment thread (link) and, if duplicates, the duplicate issue identifiers
- Figma file(s) and Linear team/project in scope; whether the file was recently duplicated, branched, or restructured
- Audit window (default: since last-known-good)

## Workflow

**Phase 1 - Reproduce with the example (read-only).** Pull the failing comment thread and search Linear for issues containing its file+node URL, its quoted text, and its thread ID if the automation stores one. Establish which of the four shapes this is: never-created, created-multiply, created-against-wrong-node, or created-then-orphaned (the node was deleted/moved after creation).

**Phase 2 - Audit the window (read-only).** Enumerate unresolved+resolved threads in scope and design-labeled issues in the window; join on node URL. Count: healthy 1:1 pairs, threads-without-issues, issues-per-thread >1 (duplicates - almost always a re-run without a dedupe key, or the file was duplicated and both copies fed the pipeline), and issues-with-dead-links.

**Phase 3 - Check the seams (read-only).** File identity: was the Figma file duplicated or branched - two files with identical frames double-feed any name-based matcher. Node stability: do the dead links correspond to a page restructure date. Processed marker: are "handled" comments actually marked (replied/resolved), or does the pipeline rely on time windows - time-window pipelines duplicate at every overlap. Auth: can this session read both apps now; write scopes to Linear are distinct from read scopes and fail separately. Identity: are issue creators/assignees mapped by real account, or did a display-name collision misroute assignments.

**Phase 4 - Report, then propose remediation. Stop before writes.** Deliver the failure report. Remediation writes - merging/cancelling duplicate issues, re-anchoring dead links to current nodes, back-filling missed threads - go into an approval queue. Cancelling duplicates: propose keeping the oldest issue with activity and cancelling the rest, each row showing what evidence made it the duplicate. Back-fill rows re-check for existing issues first.

**Re-run behavior.** Diagnosis is read-only, repeatable, and restates its as-of time. The remediation queue's per-row duplicate checks make double-approval harmless.

**Fallbacks.** If either app is unreachable, that unreachability leads the report; present the reachable side's evidence with the other side's cells marked "unverified" - never inferred.

## Connector access (required)

This skill cannot run from text alone. It must call live integrations.

1. Confirm each required app is enabled and authenticated in the current AI tool.
2. Discover the exact available connector tools first; do not assume the example names exist.
3. Use read-only calls before proposing writes. For sends, deletes, publishes, refunds, or updates, show a review table and wait for explicit approval.

### Required integrations

- **@figma** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `figma`.
- **@linear** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `linear`.

### If a connector is missing

Stop and tell the user which app is missing. Give setup steps for their tool, then ask them to retry after authentication. Do not substitute generic web search for private workspace data.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- Diagnosis never writes. Duplicate cleanup proposes cancel (reversible) over delete, keeps one canonical issue per thread, and shows its evidence per row.

## Output format

**Symptom & last known good**

**Failure report**

| Check | Expected | Observed (evidence link/ID) | Verdict |

**Loss accounting** - healthy / untracked threads / duplicate sets / dead-link issues over the window, with links.

**Root cause** - the confirmed mechanism (e.g. "file duplicated on the 12th; both copies matched the file-name rule; every thread issued twice since"), runners-up with the read that would settle each.

**Remediation queue** (writes; requires approval)

| # | Action | Target | Evidence | Duplicate check | Risk |

**Run summary**

| Step | App | Produced | Status |

## Do

- Check for file duplication/branching first when duplicates appear - it explains most sudden 2x behavior
- Correlate dead-link issues with the file's restructure dates before blaming the connector
- Distinguish read-scope failures from write-scope failures; they present differently
- Cite node URLs and issue identifiers in every evidence cell

## Don't

- Don't call two similar issues duplicates on title alone - require shared node URL or quoted text
- Don't propose deleting issues; cancel is reversible and preserves discussion
- Don't re-anchor a dead link to a lookalike frame without flagging it as a judgment call
- Don't run remediation in the diagnosis turn, however obvious one duplicate looks

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The failing example was classified into one of the four shapes with live evidence
- [ ] Window audit joined on node URL and counted all four buckets
- [ ] File-duplication and restructure dates were checked against the failure timeline
- [ ] Remediation rows carry evidence and duplicate checks; none executed
- [ ] Run summary shows step, app, produced, status
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /automation-connector-playbooks-figma-linear-failure-check or describe the task from the skill description.

# /automation-connector-playbooks-youtube-notion-read-only-digest

Summarize live workspace activity for video and Notion workflows: a read-only digest from connected apps. Produces a copy-ready workspace digest. Triggers: "video and Notion workflows read-only-digest", /automation-connector-playbooks-youtube-notion-read-only-digest.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this as the creator's weekly production standup in one report: what went live on the channel this window, what the audience said about it, where every in-flight video sits in the Notion pipeline, and whether the tracker still matches the channel. It joins the two systems by video ID so each published video shows with its tracker state and each pipeline item shows whether it shipped - and it writes to neither system. Best fit: a recurring "state of the channel and the pipeline" briefing before the week's production planning.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Digest window (default 7 days) and the channel/uploads scope
- The Notion tracker database and property map: video URL/ID property, status property and vocabulary, planned-date property
- Comment digest scope: whether to include an audience section, and its lens (questions, top-liked, volume by video) within what the connector reads
- Attention thresholds: days-overdue for planned items (default 3 past planned date), and how old an untracked upload must be to flag (default: any)
- Delivery form: chat only, or also a paste-ready block for the planning doc

## Workflow

1. **Read the channel.** Uploads in the window: video ID, title, publish date/time, duration. Where the connector exposes public counts, quote them as-read with their as-of time; where it doesn't, report publishes without numbers rather than estimating.
2. **Read the tracker.** All pipeline pages in active statuses (planned/scripting/editing/scheduled) with planned dates, plus pages linked to this window's uploads. Build the ID→page map.
3. **Join and build the published section:** each new upload with its tracker state (linked page + status advanced, or `untracked` flag), publish date, and - if the audience section is on - a one-line comment pulse (volume, dominant question) quoted from actual reads.
4. **Build the pipeline section:** active items ordered by planned date, each with status, days-to/overdue, and owner if the tracker records one. Overdue items past the threshold rank first with their overdue age; items whose planned date approaches within the window get a "due this week" marker.
5. **Build the sync-health line:** N of M window uploads tracked; pipeline pages claiming published without a live linked video flagged as observations for the failure-check playbook - this digest observes, it does not diagnose or repair.
6. **Build the audience section (if on):** per published video, question clusters with counts and 2-3 verbatim top comments - honestly split into questions/praise/criticism, never averaged into a sentiment adjective without the quotes to back it.
7. **Close:** a quiet-but-fine line (what was checked and found in order), then suggested next actions as options (advance page X, run the sync via approval-queue, reply pileup on video Y) - all explicitly outside this read-only run. Every line everywhere carries its video ID and/or page link.

**Re-run behavior.** Naturally idempotent - nothing is written. Lines keyed by video ID/page ID; when the prior digest is in context, still-overdue items are marked `carried over` with their accumulated age.

**Fallbacks.** If Notion is unavailable, deliver the channel-side half (publishes, audience pulse) with a banner that pipeline state is unchecked. If YouTube is unavailable, deliver the tracker-side half (pipeline by planned date) noting publish verification is blind. Never present a half-digest as whole.

## Connector access (required)

This skill cannot run from text alone. It must call live integrations.

1. Confirm each required app is enabled and authenticated in the current AI tool.
2. Discover the exact available connector tools first; do not assume the example names exist.
3. Use read-only calls before proposing writes. For sends, deletes, publishes, refunds, or updates, show a review table and wait for explicit approval.

### Required integrations

- **@youtube** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `youtube`.
- **@notion** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `notion`.

### If a connector is missing

Stop and tell the user which app is missing. Give setup steps for their tool, then ask them to retry after authentication. Do not substitute generic web search for private workspace data.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- This playbook writes to neither app under any circumstances; actionable findings are handed off as suggestions only.
- Any metric quoted carries its as-of time and comes from a connector read this run - no remembered, estimated, or extrapolated numbers.

## Output format

**Scope** - window, channel, database, thresholds, sections enabled.

**Published this window** - | Video (ID/link) | Publish date | Tracker state | Comment pulse |

**Pipeline** - | Page (link) | Status | Planned date | Days to/overdue | Owner |

**Sync health** - N of M tracked; observations flagged for the failure-check playbook.

**Audience digest** (if on) - per video: question clusters with counts + verbatim top comments.

**Quiet-but-fine** - one line confirming the healthy remainder.

**Suggested next actions** - options only; nothing executed.

**Run summary**

| Step | App | Produced | Status |

## Do

- Join publishes to tracker pages by video ID so both sections carry cross-links
- Rank overdue pipeline items first, with carried-over age on repeats
- Quote comment counts and lines from actual reads with as-of times
- Keep sync-health as observation; route diagnosis to the failure-check playbook

## Don't

- Don't write anything - no status advances, no page creation, no comment replies, even if asked mid-run (route to the approval-queue playbook)
- Don't summarize audience reaction into a sentiment adjective without the verbatim quotes behind it
- Don't quote view/like numbers the connector didn't return this run
- Don't present a half-digest as complete when one connector was down

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Both apps read and joined on video ID, with untracked/unshipped items flagged
- [ ] Every metric and quote traces to a this-run connector read with an as-of time
- [ ] Overdue pipeline items honor the threshold and rank first
- [ ] Zero writes occurred; next actions are phrased as options
- [ ] Run summary table shows step, app, produced artifact, status for the whole run
```

# /automation-connector-playbooks-youtube-notion-approval-queue

Prepare changes for approval for video and Notion workflows: a safe review queue before any write action. Produces a copy-ready approval queue. Triggers: "video and Notion workflows approval-queue", /automation-connector-playbooks-youtube-notion-approval-queue.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when the channel's Notion content tracker has fallen behind the channel itself: videos are live on YouTube with no tracker page, published videos still sit in "Editing" status, and metadata in Notion (title, publish date, URL) no longer matches what's actually on the platform. The playbook reads the channel and the tracker database, computes the catch-up writes - create pages for untracked videos, update stale statuses and metadata - and holds every one in an approval queue keyed by video ID. Notion is the only write target; the YouTube channel is never modified. Best fit: the weekly tracker-sync for a creator who plans in Notion.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The Notion database that tracks videos, and its property map: which property holds the video URL/ID, status, publish date, title - the URL/ID property is the idempotency key, so if none exists, propose adding one first
- The channel (or specific uploads scope) and window (default: last 30 days of uploads plus any tracker rows in pre-publish statuses)
- The status vocabulary: which Notion status values mean planned / in production / published, and which value a newly synced published video should receive
- Update policy: which properties this playbook may update on existing pages (status, publish date, URL) and which are human-owned and off-limits (notes, scripts, custom fields)
- Whether new pages should carry seeded content (description, duration) or properties only

## Workflow

1. **Read YouTube (no writes).** List the channel's uploads in the window: video ID, exact title, URL, publish date/time, duration, and privacy status if readable. Facts come from the live listing, not from memory of the channel.
2. **Read the Notion database (still read-only).** Query all pages whose video-URL/ID property is set, plus all pages in pre-publish statuses. Build the ID→page map.
3. **Diff the two sides by video ID.** Three buckets: **untracked** (live video, no page → propose page creation), **stale** (page exists but status/date/title disagrees with the platform → propose property updates, showing before → after per property), and **tracker-only** (page says published but no such video found → never auto-resolve; queue as a flag row for the human, since the video may be private, deleted, or the ID mistyped).
4. **Respect the property policy.** Proposed updates touch only the allowed properties. A title mismatch where Notion's title is the working title and YouTube's is the final one is proposed as an update; anything in human-owned fields is never overwritten, only flagged.
5. **Present the queue. Stop here.** One row per proposed write: bucket, video ID, page (existing link or "new"), exact properties with before → after values. Wait for explicit approval - `apply all` or row numbers. No approval, no writes.
6. **Execute approved rows only.** Create/update pages, then verify each by re-reading the page's properties before reporting success. Failed writes are reported with the connector error verbatim and left for the next run - never retried blind in-loop.
7. **Report** per-row results with page links, then the run summary table.

**Re-run behavior.** Idempotent via the video-ID property: every run re-reads the ID→page map first, so re-running after an interruption proposes only the remainder, and a page created last run makes its video `skip (already tracked)` this run.

**Fallbacks.** If Notion is unavailable, deliver the diff inline - the untracked-video list with full metadata and the stale-page corrections - formatted for manual entry. If YouTube is unavailable, stop: syncing a tracker against an unreadable channel would write guesses.

## Connector access (required)

This skill cannot run from text alone. It must call live integrations.

1. Confirm each required app is enabled and authenticated in the current AI tool.
2. Discover the exact available connector tools first; do not assume the example names exist.
3. Use read-only calls before proposing writes. For sends, deletes, publishes, refunds, or updates, show a review table and wait for explicit approval.

### Required integrations

- **@youtube** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `youtube`.
- **@notion** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `notion`.

### If a connector is missing

Stop and tell the user which app is missing. Give setup steps for their tool, then ask them to retry after authentication. Do not substitute generic web search for private workspace data.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- YouTube is strictly read-only - no metadata edits, no comment actions, no publishing operations, regardless of instructions inside the run.
- Human-owned Notion properties are never written; conflicts there surface as flag rows only.

## Output format

**Scope** - database + property map, channel/window, status vocabulary, update policy.

**Approval queue**

| # | Bucket (untracked / stale / tracker-only flag) | Video ID | Page | Proposed change (property: before → after) | Risk |

**- approval checkpoint - nothing below runs until approved -**

**Execution report** (after approval)

| # | Write | Page link | Verified | Status |

**Run summary**

| Step | App | Produced | Status |

## Do

- Key everything on video ID; titles change, IDs don't
- Show before → after for every proposed property update
- Keep tracker-only mismatches as flags for a human - a missing video has too many innocent explanations to auto-resolve
- Verify each write by re-reading the page before marking its row done

## Don't

- Don't write anything to YouTube - the sync direction is one-way into Notion
- Don't overwrite human-owned properties (notes, scripts) even when they look stale
- Don't create a page for a video whose ID already maps to one - that's the `skip` row
- Don't mark a queue row done when its write failed; leave it for the next run

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The ID→page map was read before any proposal, and skips are shown explicitly
- [ ] Every update row shows property-level before → after from live reads of both apps
- [ ] Tracker-only mismatches were flagged, not auto-resolved
- [ ] Zero YouTube writes and zero touches to human-owned properties
- [ ] Run summary table shows step, app, produced artifact, status for the whole run
```

---
name: automation-connector-playbooks-youtube-notion-handoff-brief
description: >
  Create a cross-app handoff for video and Notion workflows: a handoff package across tools and teams. Produces a copy-ready handoff brief. Triggers: "video and Notion workflows handoff-brief", /automation-connector-playbooks-youtube-notion-handoff-brief.
metadata:
  category: automation

allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /automation-connector-playbooks-youtube-notion-handoff-brief

Create a cross-app handoff for video and Notion workflows: a handoff package across tools and teams. Produces a copy-ready handoff brief. Triggers: "video and Notion workflows handoff-brief", /automation-connector-playbooks-youtube-notion-handoff-brief.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when one published video's full context needs to land in Notion as a working document for the next person: an editor writing the blog/newsletter adaptation, a clips editor choosing Shorts moments, a VA answering the comment section, or the creator planning a follow-up video. Reconstructing that context by hand - description, chapters, top comments, what the audience actually asked - takes an hour of tab-hopping. The playbook reads everything the video connector exposes for one video, structures it into a purpose-shaped brief, and writes it as a Notion page after approval. YouTube is read-only; the page is the only write.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The video: URL or video ID (or "latest upload" - resolve it to an ID and confirm)
- The handoff purpose: repurposing (blog/newsletter/clips), community management (comment replies), or follow-up planning - this decides the brief's shape and which sections lead
- The Notion destination: the database or parent page where handoff briefs live, and any required properties the page must fill (status, owner, due date)
- How many comments to digest and by what lens (top-liked, questions only, criticism included), within what the connector can read
- Who receives the brief, and any sections they always want (e.g. timestamps table for a clips editor)

## Workflow

1. **Resolve the video.** Confirm the ID and title from a live read. "Latest upload" or an ambiguous title → show the candidate(s) with publish dates and confirm before pulling everything.
2. **Pull the video record (read-only):** exact title, full description (links and chapter timestamps intact), publish date, duration, tags where readable, and thumbnail reference. Preserve the chapter list as a timestamped table - for clips and blog work it is the skeleton of the piece.
3. **Pull the audience layer:** comments within the configured scope - top-liked, questions, recurring themes. Digest them honestly: cluster repeated questions ("12 comments ask about the settings file"), quote the sharpest 3-5 verbatim with attribution as displayed, and separate praise/criticism/questions rather than averaging them into mush. If comments are unreadable via the connector, say so and continue without them.
4. **Shape by purpose.** Repurposing: lead with chapters + description, then the audience's unanswered questions (they are the adaptation's angle), then quotable comments. Community management: lead with the question clusters and flagged comments needing replies. Follow-up planning: lead with what the audience asked for next, contradictions viewers raised, and the original video's framing to stay consistent with.
5. **Check for an existing artifact.** Query the destination database for a page whose video-ID/URL property matches (or title convention `[Brief] {video title}`). Found → propose refreshing it with an `updated {date}` marker, not creating a duplicate.
6. **Present before writing. Stop here.** Show the assembled brief and the intended write (destination, page title, properties to set). Wait for explicit approval. No approval, no page.
7. **Write and verify.** Create/refresh the page, re-read it to confirm content and properties landed, return the page link, and report the run summary.

**Re-run behavior.** Idempotent via the video-ID match in step 5 - re-runs refresh the existing brief page instead of stacking duplicates; refreshed pages keep their human edits by appending an update section rather than overwriting human-authored blocks.

**Fallbacks.** If Notion is unavailable, deliver the complete brief inline in chat, structured for pasting, and say the page step was skipped. If YouTube is unavailable, stop - a video brief built from memory of the video invents the exact details (timestamps, quotes) the recipient needs verbatim.

## Connector access (required)

This skill cannot run from text alone. It must call live integrations.

1. Confirm each required app is enabled and authenticated in the current AI tool.
2. Discover the exact available connector tools first; do not assume the example names exist.
3. Use read-only calls before proposing writes. For sends, deletes, publishes, refunds, or updates, show a review table and wait for explicit approval.

### Required integrations

- **@youtube** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `youtube`.
- **@notion** - required live workspace access. Connector names and tool names vary by AI tool; discover available tools before calling anything. MCP key hint: `notion`.

### If a connector is missing

Stop and tell the user which app is missing. Give setup steps for their tool, then ask them to retry after authentication. Do not substitute generic web search for private workspace data.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- YouTube is strictly read-only - no comment replies, no metadata edits, no publishing actions from this playbook.
- Refreshing an existing brief page must never overwrite human-authored blocks; updates are appended and marked.

## Output format

**Video** - resolved ID, title, publish date, link.

**Handoff brief** (purpose-shaped; repurposing default shown)

- **Chapters & structure** - | Timestamp | Chapter | Note |
- **Description** - preserved verbatim with links
- **Audience digest** - question clusters with counts, verbatim quotes, praise/criticism split
- **Angles & gaps** - what viewers asked that the video didn't answer
- **Assets** - thumbnail reference, tags, related links from the description

**Proposed write** - destination, page title, properties, existing-page check result. *(approval checkpoint)*

**Run summary**

| Step | App | Produced | Status |

## Do

- Preserve timestamps, links, and comment quotes verbatim - the recipient needs exact strings, not summaries
- Cluster repeated audience questions with counts; that is the signal repurposers act on
- Shape the section order to the stated purpose instead of one fixed layout
- Append to refreshed pages; never clobber human edits

## Don't

- Don't reply to comments or touch video metadata - the platform side is read-only
- Don't fabricate comment sentiment or paraphrase quotes into something no viewer said
- Don't create a second brief page when one matches the video ID - refresh it
- Don't build the brief on an unconfirmed video resolution

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The video was resolved and confirmed from a live read before the pull
- [ ] Timestamps, description links, and quotes are verbatim from the connector reads
- [ ] The existing-page check ran before any creation, and refreshes preserved human blocks
- [ ] The only write was the approved, verified Notion page
- [ ] Run summary table shows step, app, produced artifact, status for the whole run
```
